For many Senegalese children, the Atlantic Ocean is no longer just the sea — it is where their parents disappeared.

In this opinion piece, Ugandan writer and development practitioner Simpson Muhwezi argues that Europe’s response to African migration cannot stop at patrol boats and border funding, warning that thousands of families are being left behind to deal with trauma, poverty and unanswered grief.

#GSWOpinion #Senegal #Migration #Africa #Europe

🚨Breaking News🚨
⚽️WC Chronicles⚽️
🔥Daily Briefing 🔥
🇫🇷 Group I 🇫🇷
🎆May 10, 2026🎆
France🇫🇷Senegal🇸🇳 Iraq🇮🇶 Norway🇳🇴

33 DAYS TO GO:
🔥BREAKING NEWS 🔥– 
May 10, 2026
Mbappé Issues Rallying Cry: “This Group Is Ours to Conquer”
According to Soccer24 and major newspapers (Marca, The Guardian, AS, and Goal), Group I training camps are erupting with superstar fire. France captain Kylian Mbappé has been leading passionate players-only huddles, declaring, “This group is ours to conquer — we are not here to survive, we are here to dominate.” 
The raw emotion and leadership have sent shockwaves through Norway, Senegal, and Iraq camps, who now know France are arriving with ruthless title-winning mentality.

Hot Video Highlighting Today’s Breaking News:
Watch this explosive Group I preview that perfectly captures Mbappé’s leadership moment and France’s attacking fire: 🔥📽️
https://t.co/R6jSpNb5mN 

FIREPOWER RANKINGS: GROUP I Quick Visual – Offensive Firepower Table 
🔥GROUP I OFFENSIVE FIREPOWER RANKINGS 🔥

1. 🇫🇷FRANCE – 94%
Poss: 63% | Shots: 15.2 | Saves Forced: 5.7 | Corners: 6.9
⚡World-class speed + rotations

2. 🇳🇴NORWAY – 80%
Poss: 55% | Shots: 13.4 | Saves Forced: 4.8 | Corners: 5.6
⚡Creative midfield + Ødegaard magic

3. 🇸🇳SENEGAL – 76%
Poss: 50% | Shots: 12.1 | Saves Forced: 4.3 | Corners: 5.4
💪 Physicality + counter power

4. 🇮🇶IRAQ – 66%
Poss: 47% | Shots: 10.8 | Saves Forced: 3.9 | Corners: 4.9
⚡Passionate runs + counter threat

What is Offensive Elo?
Offensive Elo measures a team’s attacking strength based on results against quality opposition, translated into a percentage (higher % = stronger attack).

1. 🇫🇷France 🇫🇷– Offensive Elo 94% Avg 
Possession: 63% • Shots per game: 15.2 • Saves forced: 5.7 • Corners won: 6.9

BeSoccer attributes: 
World-class dribbling and vision in tight spaces, lethal low-driven counters, clinical one-touch finishing inside the box, elite set-piece delivery, and constant positional rotations that create overloads and confusion.
Projected Formation: 
4-2-3-1 
(fluid attacking midfield with false-9 rotations).
Squad fit: 
This shape lets Kylian Mbappé roam freely, allows Griezmann and Dembélé to interchange, and gives Tchouaméni and Kanté control in midfield — perfectly matching France’s depth of technical attackers and Deschamps’ high-tempo philosophy.
Defense challenge: 
Norway’s quick transitions could catch France on the break if they overcommit.

Additional takeaways: 
(1) France generates the most clear-cut chances in the group; 
(2) Their corner delivery is the deadliest set-piece weapon; 
(3) Mbappé’s individual brilliance regularly forces fouls in dangerous areas; 
(4) Late-game substitute impact keeps their attacking intensity unmatched.

2. 🇳🇴Norway🇳🇴 – Offensive Elo 80% Avg 
Possession: 55% • Shots per game: 13.4 • Saves forced: 4.8 • Corners won: 5.6

BeSoccer attributes: 
Creative midfield play, dangerous set-pieces, strong individual dribbling, clinical counter-attacking, and intelligent movement that stretches opposing defenses.
Projected Formation: 
4-2-3-1 
(compact with creative No. 10).
Squad fit: 
Allows Martin Ødegaard to dictate from midfield, Haaland to dominate as the focal point, and allows wide players to exploit half-spaces — ideal for Norway’s technical and organized squad.
Defense challenge: 
France’s fluid rotations could overload Norway’s midfield and create gaps.

Additional takeaways: 
(1) Ødegaard’s vision creates numerous scoring opportunities; 
(2) Set-piece threat is consistent; 
(3) Individual flair often unlocks tight defenses; 
(4) Counter speed remains a major weapon.

3. 🇸🇳Senegal🇸🇳 – 
Offensive Elo 76% Avg 
Possession: 50% • Shots per game: 12.1 • Saves forced: 4.3 • Corners won: 5.4

BeSoccer attributes: 
Physical presence and aerial dominance up front, powerful set-piece threat from dead balls, direct attacking runs, relentless pressing to win second balls, and never-say-die spirit that creates late-game opportunities.
Projected Formation: 
4-4-2 diamond 
(compact and physical).
Squad fit: The diamond gives Kalidou Koulibaly freedom to surge forward, while the two strikers exploit second balls and aerial duels — perfectly suiting Senegal’s high-energy, combative squad.
Defense challenge: 
France’s high-tempo rotations could overload Senegal’s midfield.
Additional takeaways: 
(1) Set-pieces are Senegal’s biggest weapon — they lead the group in corner goals; 
(2) Relentless physical intensity wears down opponents late; 
(3) Direct style forces defensive mistakes and turnovers;
(4) Never-say-die comeback mentality frequently 
delivers late goals.

4.🇮🇶 Iraq 🇮🇶– 
Offensive Elo 66% Avg 
Possession: 47% • Shots per game: 10.8 • Saves forced: 3.9 • Corners won: 4.9

BeSoccer attributes: 
Passionate attacking runs with individual flair, dangerous counter-attacking threat, quick transitions through the middle, strong aerial presence, and emotional leadership that inspires moments of brilliance.
Projected Formation: 
4-2-3-1 
(compact and organized).
Squad fit: 
The formation allows Ali Adnan to act as a creative hub while giving forward players room to roam — matching Iraq’s athletic and spirited squad.
Defense challenge: 
Senegal’s physical 4-4-2 diamond could overpower Iraq in duels.
Additional takeaways: 
(1) Iraq force more saves than their possession suggests; (2) Quick transitions often catch bigger teams off guard; (3) Emotional intensity creates unpredictable moments of magic; 
(4) Counter-attacking speed remains a constant threat even when trailing.

🗝️KEY GAME STATS TO WATCH IN GROUP I🗝️
These are the most important metrics that will decide who advances — and why they matter:
🅰️Saves forced on opponents – The team that forces the most saves usually creates the highest-quality chances. France currently leads the group here.

🅱️Corners won – Corners are the hidden weapon in this group. France and Senegal both average over 5.4 per game — a single well-delivered corner could decide a tight match.

🔠Shots per game vs possession – Raw possession doesn’t win games; effective shots do. Norway’s ability to generate high shots on moderate possession shows their lethal counter threat.

🔠Late-match physical intensity – Teams that maintain or increase shots and pressing after the 70th minute (Senegal and Iraq) often steal points when others tire.

🎲WHO IS FAVORED TO TOP GROUP I? 🎲
(Updated May 10)

• 🇫🇷France: 65% – Highest offensive Elo + elite leadership

• 🇳🇴Norway: 18% – Ødegaard’s creativity makes them dangerous contenders

• 🇸🇳Senegal: 12% – Koulibaly’s steel could spark an upset

• 🇮🇶Iraq: 5% – Passionate underdogs, but steepest climb

 Additional takeaways:
(1) France’s fluid rotations create constant overloads that no other team in the group can currently match.
(2) Norway’s ability to force turnovers high up the pitch makes them the most likely to punish any French complacency.

🎤MY TAKE🎤
Mbappé’s rallying cry has completely changed the temperature of Group I. France’s attacking hunger and tactical mastery could turn this group into their playground. The depth is frightening, and Deschamps’ leadership gives them the clearest edge. 

Norway brings creative danger, but France’s superior offensive Elo and the leaked championship mentality currently make them the heavy favorites to crush the group. The real fireworks will explode if Ødegaard, Koulibaly or Adnan can summon a moment of leadership magic to stop the French machine.

📢GROUP I DYNAMICS ANALYSIS📢
Group I is a powder keg ready to ignite: a superstar-laden European giant collides with a rising Scandinavian side, a physically dominant African team, and a tactically sharp Asian underdog. The battle is superstar quality vs youthful creativity vs defensive steel vs underdog heart — and the stakes couldn’t be higher.

Key Dynamics 
France are the clear favorites with the highest offensive Elo.
Norway are the most dangerous challengers thanks to Ødegaard’s vision.
Senegal is the tactical dark horse — disciplined and hard to break down.
Iraq bring passion but face the steepest climb.

Bottom Line: 
France is expected to dominate Group I, but the fight for second place between Norway and Senegal will be decided by leadership and mentality more than raw talent. Mbappé vs Ødegaard is the duel that could define the group — and it promises absolute fireworks.

 Watch this explosive Group I preview that perfectly matches our Offensive Firepower Rankings and shows exactly why these teams will decide who advances from this stacked group: 🔥📽️
https://t.co/uwTjdDKphD Big Takeaway for 2026
After ranking every team’s offensive firepower in Group I with hard data, one truth stands out: talent gets you to the World Cup, but leadership + championship mentality can turn a dominant attack into an unstoppable force.
 France currently holds the crown in Group I.

🔥⁉️The Big Question:

🚨Breaking News🚨
⚽️WC Chronicles⚽️
🔥Daily Briefing 🔥
🇫🇷 Group I 🇫🇷
🎆May 10, 2026🎆
France🇫🇷Senegal🇸🇳 Iraq🇮🇶 Norway🇳🇴

33 DAYS TO GO:
🔥BREAKING NEWS 🔥– 
May 10, 2026
Mbappé Issues Rallying Cry: “This Group Is Ours to Conquer”
According to Soccer24 and major newspapers (Marca, The Guardian, AS, and Goal), Group I training camps are erupting with superstar fire. France captain Kylian Mbappé has been leading passionate players-only huddles, declaring, “This group is ours to conquer — we are not here to survive, we are here to dominate.” 
The raw emotion and leadership have sent shockwaves through Norway, Senegal, and Iraq camps, who now know France are arriving with ruthless title-winning mentality.

Hot Video Highlighting Today’s Breaking News:
Watch this explosive Group I preview that perfectly captures Mbappé’s leadership moment and France’s attacking fire: 🔥📽️
https://t.co/R6jSpNb5mN 

FIREPOWER RANKINGS: GROUP I Quick Visual – Offensive Firepower Table 
🔥GROUP I OFFENSIVE FIREPOWER RANKINGS 🔥

1. 🇫🇷FRANCE – 94%
Poss: 63% | Shots: 15.2 | Saves Forced: 5.7 | Corners: 6.9
⚡World-class speed + rotations

2. 🇳🇴NORWAY – 80%
Poss: 55% | Shots: 13.4 | Saves Forced: 4.8 | Corners: 5.6
⚡Creative midfield + Ødegaard magic

3. 🇸🇳SENEGAL – 76%
Poss: 50% | Shots: 12.1 | Saves Forced: 4.3 | Corners: 5.4
💪 Physicality + counter power

4. 🇮🇶IRAQ – 66%
Poss: 47% | Shots: 10.8 | Saves Forced: 3.9 | Corners: 4.9
⚡Passionate runs + counter threat

What is Offensive Elo?
Offensive Elo measures a team’s attacking strength based on results against quality opposition, translated into a percentage (higher % = stronger attack).

1. 🇫🇷France 🇫🇷– Offensive Elo 94% Avg 
Possession: 63% • Shots per game: 15.2 • Saves forced: 5.7 • Corners won: 6.9

BeSoccer attributes: 
World-class dribbling and vision in tight spaces, lethal low-driven counters, clinical one-touch finishing inside the box, elite set-piece delivery, and constant positional rotations that create overloads and confusion.
Projected Formation: 
4-2-3-1 
(fluid attacking midfield with false-9 rotations).
Squad fit: 
This shape lets Kylian Mbappé roam freely, allows Griezmann and Dembélé to interchange, and gives Tchouaméni and Kanté control in midfield — perfectly matching France’s depth of technical attackers and Deschamps’ high-tempo philosophy.
Defense challenge: 
Norway’s quick transitions could catch France on the break if they overcommit.

Additional takeaways: 
(1) France generates the most clear-cut chances in the group; 
(2) Their corner delivery is the deadliest set-piece weapon; 
(3) Mbappé’s individual brilliance regularly forces fouls in dangerous areas; 
(4) Late-game substitute impact keeps their attacking intensity unmatched.

2. 🇳🇴Norway🇳🇴 – Offensive Elo 80% Avg 
Possession: 55% • Shots per game: 13.4 • Saves forced: 4.8 • Corners won: 5.6

BeSoccer attributes: 
Creative midfield play, dangerous set-pieces, strong individual dribbling, clinical counter-attacking, and intelligent movement that stretches opposing defenses.
Projected Formation: 
4-2-3-1 
(compact with creative No. 10).
Squad fit: 
Allows Martin Ødegaard to dictate from midfield, Haaland to dominate as the focal point, and allows wide players to exploit half-spaces — ideal for Norway’s technical and organized squad.
Defense challenge: 
France’s fluid rotations could overload Norway’s midfield and create gaps.

Additional takeaways: 
(1) Ødegaard’s vision creates numerous scoring opportunities; 
(2) Set-piece threat is consistent; 
(3) Individual flair often unlocks tight defenses; 
(4) Counter speed remains a major weapon.

3. 🇸🇳Senegal🇸🇳 – 
Offensive Elo 76% Avg 
Possession: 50% • Shots per game: 12.1 • Saves forced: 4.3 • Corners won: 5.4

BeSoccer attributes: 
Physical presence and aerial dominance up front, powerful set-piece threat from dead balls, direct attacking runs, relentless pressing to win second balls, and never-say-die spirit that creates late-game opportunities.
Projected Formation: 
4-4-2 diamond 
(compact and physical).
Squad fit: The diamond gives Kalidou Koulibaly freedom to surge forward, while the two strikers exploit second balls and aerial duels — perfectly suiting Senegal’s high-energy, combative squad.
Defense challenge: 
France’s high-tempo rotations could overload Senegal’s midfield.
Additional takeaways: 
(1) Set-pieces are Senegal’s biggest weapon — they lead the group in corner goals; 
(2) Relentless physical intensity wears down opponents late; 
(3) Direct style forces defensive mistakes and turnovers;
(4) Never-say-die comeback mentality frequently 
delivers late goals.

4.🇮🇶 Iraq 🇮🇶– 
Offensive Elo 66% Avg 
Possession: 47% • Shots per game: 10.8 • Saves forced: 3.9 • Corners won: 4.9

BeSoccer attributes: 
Passionate attacking runs with individual flair, dangerous counter-attacking threat, quick transitions through the middle, strong aerial presence, and emotional leadership that inspires moments of brilliance.
Projected Formation: 
4-2-3-1 
(compact and organized).
Squad fit: 
The formation allows Ali Adnan to act as a creative hub while giving forward players room to roam — matching Iraq’s athletic and spirited squad.
Defense challenge: 
Senegal’s physical 4-4-2 diamond could overpower Iraq in duels.
Additional takeaways: 
(1) Iraq force more saves than their possession suggests; (2) Quick transitions often catch bigger teams off guard; (3) Emotional intensity creates unpredictable moments of magic; 
(4) Counter-attacking speed remains a constant threat even when trailing.

🗝️KEY GAME STATS TO WATCH IN GROUP I🗝️
These are the most important metrics that will decide who advances — and why they matter:
🅰️Saves forced on opponents – The team that forces the most saves usually creates the highest-quality chances. France currently leads the group here.

🅱️Corners won – Corners are the hidden weapon in this group. France and Senegal both average over 5.4 per game — a single well-delivered corner could decide a tight match.

🔠Shots per game vs possession – Raw possession doesn’t win games; effective shots do. Norway’s ability to generate high shots on moderate possession shows their lethal counter threat.

🔠Late-match physical intensity – Teams that maintain or increase shots and pressing after the 70th minute (Senegal and Iraq) often steal points when others tire.

🎲WHO IS FAVORED TO TOP GROUP I? 🎲
(Updated May 10)

• 🇫🇷France: 65% – Highest offensive Elo + elite leadership

• 🇳🇴Norway: 18% – Ødegaard’s creativity makes them dangerous contenders

• 🇸🇳Senegal: 12% – Koulibaly’s steel could spark an upset

• 🇮🇶Iraq: 5% – Passionate underdogs, but steepest climb

 Additional takeaways:
(1) France’s fluid rotations create constant overloads that no other team in the group can currently match.
(2) Norway’s ability to force turnovers high up the pitch makes them the most likely to punish any French complacency.

🎤MY TAKE🎤
Mbappé’s rallying cry has completely changed the temperature of Group I. France’s attacking hunger and tactical mastery could turn this group into their playground. The depth is frightening, and Deschamps’ leadership gives them the clearest edge. 

Norway brings creative danger, but France’s superior offensive Elo and the leaked championship mentality currently make them the heavy favorites to crush the group. The real fireworks will explode if Ødegaard, Koulibaly or Adnan can summon a moment of leadership magic to stop the French machine.

📢GROUP I DYNAMICS ANALYSIS📢
Group I is a powder keg ready to ignite: a superstar-laden European giant collides with a rising Scandinavian side, a physically dominant African team, and a tactically sharp Asian underdog. The battle is superstar quality vs youthful creativity vs defensive steel vs underdog heart — and the stakes couldn’t be higher.

Key Dynamics 
France are the clear favorites with the highest offensive Elo.
Norway are the most dangerous challengers thanks to Ødegaard’s vision.
Senegal is the tactical dark horse — disciplined and hard to break down.
Iraq bring passion but face the steepest climb.

Bottom Line: 
France is expected to dominate Group I, but the fight for second place between Norway and Senegal will be decided by leadership and mentality more than raw talent. Mbappé vs Ødegaard is the duel that could define the group — and it promises absolute fireworks.

 Watch this explosive Group I preview that perfectly matches our Offensive Firepower Rankings and shows exactly why these teams will decide who advances from this stacked group: 🔥📽️
https://t.co/uwTjdDKphD Big Takeaway for 2026
After ranking every team’s offensive firepower in Group I with hard data, one truth stands out: talent gets you to the World Cup, but leadership + championship mentality can turn a dominant attack into an unstoppable force.
 France currently holds the crown in Group I.

column4:
MACRON IS IN NAIROBI. HERE IS WHAT IS REALLY HAPPENING THIS WEEK.

Kenya Politics | @kenya_politics_ | May 10, 2026

Emmanuel Macron landed in Nairobi today. Thirty heads of state are either here or on their way. Sierra Leone's President Julius Maada Bio was one of the first to touch down at JKIA this morning. Tomorrow and Tuesday, KICC becomes the most watched room on the continent as Kenya and France co-host the Africa Forward Summit, billed as a new chapter in how Europe engages with Africa.

The headline framing is partnership, innovation and growth. The real story underneath it is France trying to rebuild influence on a continent that has been rejecting it in very public and humiliating ways.

Since 2020 a wave of coups across the Sahel has installed military governments that told French troops to leave and in several cases invited Russian mercenaries in to replace them. Mali gone. Burkina Faso gone. Niger gone. France handed over its last major military facility in Senegal last July after President Bassirou Diomaye Faye, who is attending this summit, said French military bases were incompatible with Senegalese sovereignty. France also lost a KSh 160 billion highway contract in Kenya last year when Ruto's government cancelled a deal with French firm Vinci and awarded it to Chinese firms instead.

That is the backdrop against which Macron has flown to Nairobi.

This is the first Africa-France summit ever held in an English-speaking country. That choice is deliberate. France's traditional Francophone relationships have become a liability and Macron is trying to rewrite the story before he leaves office in August. This summit is one of his last major international appearances as French president. He announced it alongside Ruto at the UN General Assembly in September last year. He chose Nairobi, not Paris, not Dakar, not Abidjan, for what is shaping up to be a farewell moment on the continent.

For Kenya the stakes are concrete. The Ruto administration is eyeing a KES 400 billion investment package covering transport infrastructure, green energy and the digital economy. The long-delayed Rironi-Mau Summit Road is expected to receive a final signature that unlocks construction funding. France's development agency AFD has pledged KES 65 billion for geothermal expansion at Olkaria. A French-Kenyan Tech Hub in Westlands is in the pipeline. There are over 150 French companies already doing business in Kenya employing over 70,000 Kenyans. That number is what both governments want to grow.

The summit also feeds directly into the G7 that France hosts in Evian in June, which means whatever comes out of the Nairobi Declaration on Tuesday carries more downstream weight than a typical communique.

The honest question sitting over all of this is whether Kenya gets something real or just provides the stage for a French farewell tour. Macron needs this summit for his legacy. Ruto needs it for investment. Both men have reasons to make the optics work. Whether the Nairobi Declaration on Tuesday contains concrete, funded, time-bound commitments or the usual diplomatic language that dissolves on the flight home is what Kenya Politics will be watching.

Nairobi has hosted the Africa Climate Summit, the Africa We Build Summit and now the Africa Forward Summit. The city is becoming the address for these conversations. The question is whether that translates into something Kenyans outside KICC can actually feel. 

Sources: @TheStarKenya, @NationAfrica, @DSarabic, @ReutersAfrica, @euobs | May 10 2026

column4:
Senegal’s Prime Minister Ousmane Sonko sees off President Bassirou Diomaye Faye this morning as the head of state departs for Nairobi to attend the “Africa Forward” Summit, amid growing signs of friction and internal dissent between them.

Yesterday, Senegal’s National Assembly overwhelmingly voted and approved, for the second time, electoral law amendments that paves the way for Sonko to run in the 2029 presidential election.

President Faye had declined to sign it into law over a "technical issue", and criticisms that it favours one individual.

The lawmakers have now forwarded it to President Faye for assent, putting pressure on him to either sign the reforms or risk deepening perceptions of a growing power struggle within.

Last month, Senegal’s National Assembly, where the ruling PASTEF party led by Sonko holds a commanding majority, overwhelmingly approved the bill, with the proposed measures seen as opening the door for Sonko to run.

Sonko, 51, was disqualified from the 2024 presidential race following a conviction for defaming a minister accused of embezzling public funds, which could also affect his eligibility in 2029.

Instead, he chose Faye to run in his place.

Senegal’s Prime Minister Ousmane Sonko sees off President Bassirou Diomaye Faye this morning as the head of state departs for Nairobi to attend the “Africa Forward” Summit, amid growing signs of friction and internal dissent between them.

Yesterday, Senegal’s National Assembly overwhelmingly voted and approved, for the second time, electoral law amendments that paves the way for Sonko to run in the 2029 presidential election.

President Faye had declined to sign it into law over a "technical issue", and criticisms that it favours one individual.

The lawmakers have now forwarded it to President Faye for assent, putting pressure on him to either sign the reforms or risk deepening perceptions of a growing power struggle within.

Last month, Senegal’s National Assembly, where the ruling PASTEF party led by Sonko holds a commanding majority, overwhelmingly approved the bill, with the proposed measures seen as opening the door for Sonko to run.

Sonko, 51, was disqualified from the 2024 presidential race following a conviction for defaming a minister accused of embezzling public funds, which could also affect his eligibility in 2029.

Instead, he chose Faye to run in his place.

column4:
Senegal’s President Returns Electoral Reforms to Parliament, Paving Potential Path for PM Sonko’s 2029 Bid

Senegalese President Bassirou Diomaye Faye has sent a package of electoral law amendments back to parliament for reconsideration, a move that could ultimately clear the way for Prime Minister Ousmane Sonko to contest the 2029 presidential election.

The decision, announced on Saturday, reflects ongoing political maneuvering in the West African nation following Faye’s landmark victory in 2024. The amendments are widely seen as addressing legal barriers that previously barred Sonko, Faye’s key ally and influential opposition figure, from running for president.

Parliament must now review the revised proposals. Analysts say the changes could reshape Senegal’s political landscape ahead of the next vote.

🎆May 9, 2026🎆
France🇫🇷Senegal🇸🇳 Iraq🇮🇶 Norway🇳🇴

33 DAYS TO GO:
BREAKING NEWS – May 9, 2026
According to Soccer24 and major newspapers (Marca, The Guardian, AS, and Goal), Group I training camps are absolutely electric right now. France captain Kylian Mbappé was spotted leading explosive finishing drills.

” Norway’s Martin Ødegaard turned extra midfield sessions into a masterclass of vision and control, while Senegal’s Kalidou Koulibaly and Iraq’s Ali Adnan both rallied their squads with raw never-say-die passion. No major injuries reported, but the message from every camp is loud and clear: leadership will decide who survives this stacked, high-stakes group.

🔥LEADERSHIP POWER RANKINGS:🔥
 GROUP I
After four straight days of deep-dive captain and coach breakdowns, we’ve ranked every leadership duo in Group I using a transparent system based on:

 International caps & big-game experience
Tactical impact & past clutch moments
Coaching win percentage & pedigree
Current Team Elo strength

What is Team Elo?
Team Elo is a proven rating system that measures a national team’s true strength based on results, opponent quality, and competition level. The percentages below show each team’s relative strength within Group I (higher % = stronger), with separate offensive and defensive ratings.

1.🇫🇷 France🇫🇷 – 
Kylian Mbappé & Didier Deschamps (94/100) Mbappé: 96 caps, 56 goals, 32 assists, world-class finisher and inspirational leader

Deschamps: 
63% win rate, master of high-pressure environments

Combined Elo strength:
 France leads Group I with the highest Team Elo rating of 94% 
(Offensive 93% | Defensive 95%).

Edge:
 Star captain + legendary coach with proven World Cup pedigree.

2. 🇳🇴Norway🇳🇴 – 
Martin Ødegaard & Ståle Solbakken (82/100) Ødegaard: 68 caps, 8 goals, 28 assists, creative midfield maestro

Solbakken:
 56% win rate, expert in fearless mentality
Combined Elo strength: 80%
 (Offensive 82% | Defensive 78%)

Edge:
 Dynamic leadership and quick-transition threat.

3. 🇸🇳Senegal 🇸🇳–
 Kalidou Koulibaly & Pape Thiaw (78/100) Koulibaly: 88 caps, 3 goals, 4 assists, vocal defender and rock-solid organiser

Thiaw: 64% win rate, renowned for defensive discipline

Combined Elo strength: 76% 
(Offensive 74% | Defensive 78%)

Edge: 
Strong back-line leadership and never-say-die mentality.

4.🇮🇶 Iraq🇮🇶 – 
Ali Adnan & Graham Arnold (71/100) Adnan: 92 caps, 8 goals, 14 assists, explosive full-back and emotional leader

Arnold:
59% win rate, specialist in counter-attacking setups
Combined Elo strength: 66% (Offensive 70% | Defensive 63%)

Edge:
 Passionate underdog leadership, but least big-stage experience.

💻ELO COMPARISON ACROSS GROUPS💻
 (May 9Update)
Here’s how Group I stacks up against the rest of the tournament based on the latest World Football Elo Ratings (as of May 5-7, 2026). 
All figures are relative strength percentages (100% = strongest possible team in the 32-team field), broken down into offensive and defensive components.

TOP INDIVIDUAL TEAM ELO RATINGS
 (Single-Team Strength) 

🇫🇷France (Group I): 94% (Off 93% | Def 95%) – Highest in the tournament
🇪🇸Spain (Group H): 92% (Off 93% | Def 91%) – Close second
🇦🇷Argentina (Group J): 90%
🇳🇱Netherlands (Group F): 89%
🇩🇪Germany (Group E): 88%
🇧🇷Brazil (Group C): 88%
🇺🇸United States (Group D): 84% (boosted by home advantage)

Key Takeaways Strongest Groups: 
Groups I, J, and K are stacked with elite talent and the highest Elo averages — these will be the most competitive and dramatic groups.

Most Balanced / Unpredictable:
 Group G (73% average) and Group D are the most open. Leadership and mentality will matter more than raw Elo here.

Biggest Home Advantage Impact: 
Mexico (Group A) and the United States (Group D) both gain a significant +12-14% uplift, turning mid-tier groups into genuine contenders.

Dark Horses:
Groups with strong second-place teams (Egypt in G, Uruguay in H, Japan in F) could produce major upsets.

🎲WHO IS FAVORED TO TOP GROUP I? 🎲
(Updated May 9)
• France: 65% – Highest Elo + elite leadership
• Norway: 18% – Ødegaard’s creativity makes them dangerous contenders
• Senegal: 12% – Koulibaly’s steel could spark an upset
• Iraq: 5% – Passionate underdogs, but steepest climb

🎤MY TAKE🎤
France’s leadership combination is operating on another level right now. Kylian Mbappé’s explosive presence paired with Didier Deschamps’ tactical brilliance gives them the clearest edge in Group I.
 Norway’s duo is dynamic and creative, but France’s superior Elo and proven big-game steel currently make them the heavy favourites to top the group. 
The real story will be whether Ødegaard or Koulibaly can produce a moment of leadership magic against the favourites.

🔥GROUP I DYNAMICS ANALYSIS🔥
Group I features a superstar-laden European giant against a rising Scandinavian side, a physically dominant African team, and a tactically sharp Asian underdog. 

The battle is superstar quality vs youthful creativity vs defensive steel vs underdog heart.Key Dynamics France are the overwhelming favourites with the highest Elo and strongest leadership.

Norway are the most dangerous challengers thanks to Ødegaard’s vision and Haaland’s threat.

Senegal are the tactical dark horse — disciplined, set-piece strong, and hard to break down.
Iraq bring passion and organisation but face the steepest climb.

Predicted Order – The High-Stakes Battle for Group I France (65%) – The overwhelming favourites, but the weight of expectation could become their greatest opponent.
Norway (18%) – The creative challengers, armed with Ødegaard’s genius and ready to spring a major surprise.
Senegal (12%) – The defensive dark horse, built to frustrate and strike on the counter — a genuine threat to upset the order.
Iraq (5%) – The passionate underdogs dreaming of a fairytale run, but facing the steepest climb.

Bottom Line:
 France are expected to dominate Group I, but the fight for second place between Norway and Senegal will be decided by leadership and mentality more than raw talent.
 Mbappé vs Ødegaard is the duel that could define the group — and it promises fireworks.

🇸🇳 JUST IN: President Bassirou Diomaye Faye Rejects Electoral Bill Linked To Sonko’s 2029 Ambition

Senegalese President Bassirou Diomaye Faye has rejected controversial amendments to the country’s electoral code and returned the bill to parliament for reconsideration, dealing a major political setback to Prime Minister Ousmane Sonko.

The amendments, passed by Senegal’s National Assembly on April 28 by lawmakers aligned with the ruling PASTEF coalition, would drastically reduce the types of criminal convictions capable of disqualifying candidates from elections.

The move would effectively remove the legal obstacles created by Sonko’s defamation conviction, which barred him from contesting the 2024 presidential election.

After his disqualification, Sonko backed Faye as his replacement. Faye won the presidency and later appointed Sonko as Prime Minister.

President Faye’s refusal to sign the bill is now being viewed as a clear show of authority and one of the strongest indications yet of deepening cracks within Senegal’s ruling alliance.

The presidency also cited procedural irregularities surrounding the legislation, including concerns over conflicting versions of the bill circulated during the legislative process.

The bill now returns to parliament for further action, setting the stage for a fresh political confrontation inside Senegal’s power structure.

The once-solid Faye–Sonko alliance is now openly under strain ahead of the 2029 succession battle.

Faye cutting Sonko down to size?

Senegal's President Bassirou Diomaye Faye has declined to sign electoral law amendments that paves the way for Prime Minister Ousmane Sonko to run in the 2029 presidential election, as signs of internal friction and dissension between the two leaders grow.

Last month, Senegal’s National Assembly, where the ruling PASTEF party holds a dominant majority under Sonko, overwhelmingly passed the bill, with measures seen as opening the door for Sonko to run.

Faye is asking the lawmakers to reconsider the bill.

Sonko, 51, was disqualified from the 2024 presidential race following a conviction for defaming a minister accused of embezzling public funds, which could also affect his eligibility in 2029.

Senegal’s President Faye Sends Back Electoral Reforms Aimed at Clearing Path for PM Sonko’s 2029 Bid

Dakar, Senegal – May 9, 2026** — In a surprising move that highlights emerging tensions within Senegal’s ruling coalition, President **Bassirou Diomaye Faye** has returned a set of controversial electoral law amendments to the National Assembly for reconsideration. The amendments, overwhelmingly approved by lawmakers on April 28, 2026, sought to narrow the range of criminal convictions that disqualify individuals from running for president. Under the proposed changes, ineligibility would largely be limited to serious economic offenses such as corruption and embezzlement, potentially restoring the candidacy rights of Prime Minister **Ousmane Sonko**, who was barred from the 2024 presidential race due to a defamation conviction. The bill passed with a strong majority of 128 votes in favor, 11 against, and 2 abstentions in the Pastef-dominated National Assembly. Critics, including opposition parties, described the legislation as “tailor-made” for Sonko, the influential firebrand leader who handpicked Faye as his successor in 2024 before becoming prime minister after Faye’s victory. ### Presidential Pushback President Faye’s decision to send the bill back signals a potential rift in the powerful Faye-Sonko alliance that has dominated Senegalese politics since the 2024 elections. Reports indicate Faye received conflicting versions of the adopted text and has called for a second reading or more comprehensive review of the electoral code. This action asserts presidential authority over the legislative process while raising questions about the future dynamics between the two key figures. Faye’s camp has emphasized the need for careful deliberation on reforms that could reshape the country’s democratic framework. ### Background and Implications Sonko, a popular opposition figure known for his anti-establishment stance, was convicted of defamation in a case stemming from accusations against a former government minister. That conviction rendered him ineligible for five years under the previous electoral code. The April amendments aimed to soften such restrictions, particularly for non-economic offenses. The development comes as Senegal continues its democratic consolidation following a peaceful transition of power in 2024. Observers are watching closely to see whether parliament will reintroduce the bill in revised form or if broader constitutional and electoral reforms—some already floated by the presidency—will take precedence. Opposition parties have signaled they may challenge the process at the Constitutional Council, while the situation remains fluid with potential for further political maneuvering ahead of the 2029 presidential election. This episode underscores the complex balance of power in Senegal’s young administration, where loyalty, institutional checks, and personal ambitions intersect. Further updates are expected as parliament reconvenes to address the president’s feedback.

column4:
This week, I got to hang out with Karamba Diaby, the first African-born black man elected to the German Parliament (Bundestag). 

Born in Senegal in 1961, Karamba went to East Germany on a scholarship in 1985 and became a citizen in 2001. 

He faced severe racist abuse during his tenure, including bullet holes in his office.

Elected to serve in 2013, the Chemist and Social Democrat stepped down in 2025 to focus on family and make room for younger politicians. 

(Your Kenyan MP could never!)

FREED AFTER THE AFCON CHAOS

Three Senegalese football supporters jailed in Morocco after unrest linked to the Africa Cup of Nations final have returned home to Dakar.

The fans were among 18 Senegalese supporters prosecuted over violence surrounding the final, which saw Senegal stripped of its win against Morocco after CAF awarded the hosts a 3-0 victory instead.

#GSWNews #AFCON #Senegal #Morocco #Football #AfricaCupOfNations

“WHO Confirms Hantavirus Cases Linked to Cruise Ship Exposure as Global Monitoring Intensifies”

The World Health Organization (WHO), working under the International Health Regulations (IHR), is currently coordinating international response efforts following the confirmation of multiple hantavirus cases linked to a recent exposure incident.

Swiss health authorities reported a confirmed case involving a passenger from the MV Hondius cruise ship. The individual, after receiving a health alert from the ship’s operator, voluntarily presented himself at a hospital in Zurich, where he is currently receiving medical care.

Hantavirus is a rare but serious viral infection typically transmitted through contact with infected rodents or their waste products. It can lead to severe flu-like symptoms and, in some cases, dangerous respiratory complications.

The strain involved in this outbreak has been identified as the Andes hantavirus. Laboratory confirmation was conducted by the National Institute for Communicable Diseases and Geneva University Hospitals, with additional support from Institut Pasteur de Dakar and the National Administration of Laboratories and Health Institutes.

column35:
“WHO Confirms Hantavirus Cases Linked to Cruise Ship Exposure as Global Monitoring Intensifies”

The World Health Organization (WHO), working under the International Health Regulations (IHR), is currently coordinating international response efforts following the confirmation of multiple hantavirus cases linked to a recent exposure incident.

Swiss health authorities reported a confirmed case involving a passenger from the MV Hondius cruise ship. The individual, after receiving a health alert from the ship’s operator, voluntarily presented himself at a hospital in Zurich, where he is currently receiving medical care.

Hantavirus is a rare but serious viral infection typically transmitted through contact with infected rodents or their waste products. It can lead to severe flu-like symptoms and, in some cases, dangerous respiratory complications.

The strain involved in this outbreak has been identified as the Andes hantavirus. Laboratory confirmation was conducted by the National Institute for Communicable Diseases and Geneva University Hospitals, with additional support from Institut Pasteur de Dakar and the National Administration of Laboratories and Health Institutes.

column4:
What happened to these 2 ?

Ousmane Sonko and Bassirou Diomaye Faye united under the PASTEF “rupture” agenda and became the most powerful political duo in Senegal, promising sovereignty, anti-corruption reforms, and a break from the old system.

The rift began when Sonko accused Diomaye of drifting away from that agenda through government appointments, IMF/debt handling, and leadership decisions. Sonko publicly threatened to pull PASTEF out of government, triggering backlash and fears of instability within Senegal’s ruling camp.

Since then, Diomaye has moved to reinforce presidential authority, while Sonko intensified political mobilization and backed reforms positioning himself for a possible 2029 presidential run.

#senegal #africa #politics

Senegal 🇸🇳 and Algeria 🇩🇿 have both offered support to Mali 🇲🇱 following recent deadly attacks by jihadist and separatist groups. 

Senegal’s President Bassirou Diomaye Faye said the Malian people can count on his country’s active solidarity to help restore peace and stability in the Sahel. 

Algeria’s President Abdelmadjid Tebboune stated that Algeria stands ready to assist if Bamako requests it, stressing strong ties, dialogue, and respect for Mali’s unity while opposing a military solution. 

Regional leaders warn the instability could spread to neighboring countries.

Tensions in Florence Italy 🇮🇹 have reached a boiling point as long-standing friction between local Italians and North African immigrant communities spills into the streets. While Florence is famed for its Renaissance beauty, a darker narrative of social volatility and economic strain is currently defining the city’s atmosphere in 2026.

Recent reports highlight a surge in public disorder, including violent brawls and clashes in migrant centres that have left residents feeling increasingly unsafe. These incidents have sparked a wave of protests, with local families demanding stricter security measures while migrant groups demonstrate against systemic racism and police tactics.

This is not a new phenomenon; the city has faced recurring racial violence for decades, notably the 1990 riots and the 2018 shooting of a Senegalese vendor. However, the current political climate—marked by sharp anti-immigrant rhetoric—has deepened the divide. While some cultural advocates use art to promote integration, the reality on the ground remains polarized. Authorities are currently investigating the latest escalations, calling for calm as the city struggles to balance its identity as a global tourist hub with the harsh realities of its urban crisis. 🇮🇹🌍

Egypt’s Minister of Water Resources and Irrigation, Hani Sewilam, warned that global water challenges are intensifying under the combined pressures of climate change and rapid population growth, calling for stronger international cooperation to secure water resources.
According to the ministry, Sewilam attended the opening session of the Fifth Istanbul International Water Forum and will take part in a ministerial roundtable, alongside bilateral meetings with officials from several countries.
He stressed that the forum comes at a critical moment as water systems face mounting strain, urging integrated approaches to build resilience. Sewilam underscored that transboundary cooperation is essential, emphasizing adherence to international law, trust, and mutual benefit without causing harm.
The minister highlighted the need to embed water issues within the global climate agenda and strengthen multilateral collaboration. He pointed to the 2026 UN Water Conference as a pivotal opportunity to advance the global water agenda, noting Egypt’s role in chairing one of its interactive dialogues.
“Water security is no longer a national issue alone but a shared responsibility tied to sustainable development,” Sewilam said.
On the sidelines of the forum, he met Senegal’s Minister of Water and Sanitation, Cheikh Tidiane Dièye, to discuss cooperation ahead of Senegal’s co-hosting of the 2026 UN Water Conference. Sewilam emphasized the importance of coordination between Egypt and Senegal in international water and climate forums to achieve practical outcomes.
He praised Senegal’s leadership of the African Ministers’ Council on Water (AMCOW) and reaffirmed Egypt’s commitment to working with African partners to advance water and climate priorities, including the implementation of Africa’s Water Vision 2063.

column35:
Egypt’s Minister of Water Resources and Irrigation, Hani Sewilam, warned that global water challenges are intensifying under the combined pressures of climate change and rapid population growth, calling for stronger international cooperation to secure water resources.
According to the ministry, Sewilam attended the opening session of the Fifth Istanbul International Water Forum and will take part in a ministerial roundtable, alongside bilateral meetings with officials from several countries.
He stressed that the forum comes at a critical moment as water systems face mounting strain, urging integrated approaches to build resilience. Sewilam underscored that transboundary cooperation is essential, emphasizing adherence to international law, trust, and mutual benefit without causing harm.
The minister highlighted the need to embed water issues within the global climate agenda and strengthen multilateral collaboration. He pointed to the 2026 UN Water Conference as a pivotal opportunity to advance the global water agenda, noting Egypt’s role in chairing one of its interactive dialogues.
“Water security is no longer a national issue alone but a shared responsibility tied to sustainable development,” Sewilam said.
On the sidelines of the forum, he met Senegal’s Minister of Water and Sanitation, Cheikh Tidiane Dièye, to discuss cooperation ahead of Senegal’s co-hosting of the 2026 UN Water Conference. Sewilam emphasized the importance of coordination between Egypt and Senegal in international water and climate forums to achieve practical outcomes.
He praised Senegal’s leadership of the African Ministers’ Council on Water (AMCOW) and reaffirmed Egypt’s commitment to working with African partners to advance water and climate priorities, including the implementation of Africa’s Water Vision 2063.

column4:
Senegalese President Sacks Presidency Spokesman Amid Tensions with Prime Minister

Senegalese President Bassirou Diomaye Faye has dismissed Ousseynou Ly, Minister-Counselor and spokesperson for the Presidency, in a move highlighting growing strains within the ruling PASTEF coalition.

The decision, announced via decree, comes just days after a wide-ranging presidential interview in which Faye reaffirmed his authority over Prime Minister Ousmane Sonko, stating he could remove the premier if confidence eroded. 

Ly, a longtime PASTEF loyalist and Sonko ally, had publicly recalled the head of state’s supremacy over the Prime Minister in recent remarks.

Faye appointed lawyer and coalition coordinator Abdoulaye Tine as the new spokesperson. Ly responded by affirming his gratitude to the president and unwavering commitment to PASTEF. The shake-up underscores underlying frictions in the ruling alliance nearly two years into Faye’s presidency.

column4:
Reasons Young people without political experience or training might not do well in Politics! 

Senegal's Président Bassirou Diomaye Faye and Ousmane Sonko and carried the hopes of young people across Africa; now they are going their separate ways, and it is getting pretty bad.

President Faye has indicated he's going to fire Sonko as his Prime Minister if he no longer trusted him, stating that if Sonko "remains prime minister, it is because he retains my confidence. When that is no longer the case, there will be a new prime minister."

Faye has now dismissed the spokesperson for the presidency, Ousseynou Ly, an early member of Pastef, to replace him with Abdoulaye Tine, a lawyer and an official of the Diomaye President coalition.

But as Ousseynou Ly exits, he pledges allegiance to Sonko, insisiting that his "commitment and loyalty to the transformation project championed by PASTEF, under the leadership of President Sonko, remain unwavering."

Ousmane Sonko is the leader of Pastef party.

🚨Breaking News🚨
⚽️WC Chronicles⚽️
🔥Daily Briefing 🔥
🇫🇷  Group I 🇫🇷
🎆May 5, 2026🎆
France🇫🇷 Senegal🇸🇳 Iraq🇮🇶 Norway🇳🇴

38 DAYS TO GO:
BREAKING – May 5, 2026
Didier Deschamps has confirmed France will deploy their dynamic 4-2-3-1 system for the entire 2026 campaign, naming Kylian Mbappé captain for the upcoming closed-door Clairefontaine tactical camp focused on countering high-pressing threats. 
Senegal boss Aliou Cissé announced Sadio Mané will lead their European preparation camp, while Iraq and Norway locked in final pre-tournament sessions aimed at testing Big Four-level intensity. 
Group I is officially in full battle mode!
🔥⁉️ France vs Senegal – African Lions Ready to Bite a Big Four Giant?
Group I features France as one of the undisputed Big Four favorites with star-studded attacking depth, but Senegal’s ferocious athleticism and pressing, Iraq’s disciplined structure, and Norway’s explosive Haaland-led transitions are built to punish the exact high-pressing, possession-dominant systems of Argentina, Brazil, France, and England.
 Here’s the tactical breakdown of how these challengers could realistically test (or even upset) a Big Four side.
🔥📽️Watch the styles in action → FRANCE vs SENEGAL | FIFA WORLD CUP 2026
https://t.co/XuEhvHKNIh

Group I Tactical Evolution:
 Surveying the Past 3 World Cups1. France (Regulars — appeared in all three WCs)
2014 (Didier Deschamps): Balanced 4-3-3 / 4-2-3-1 — solid structure and quick counters.
2018–2022 (Didier Deschamps): Fluid 4-2-3-1 / 4-3-3 hybrids — high pressing, midfield overloads, set-piece specialists.

🌟Current formation drivers🌟
Kylian Mbappé (105 caps, 68 goals, 32 assists – Soccer24) – The superstar forward who fuels the 4-2-3-1 transitions. His pace and 7.9+ ratings stretch every defence.
Aurélien Tchouaméni (58 caps, 5 goals, 11 assists – Soccer24) – The midfield destroyer who protects the high line. His 7.7+ ratings let France press aggressively.
Strengths:
 Elite individual quality, tactical adaptability, lethal counters and dead balls.
Drawbacks: 
Vulnerable to ultra-compact low blocks.

🇫🇷 Tactical edge vs Big Four
France’s dynamic 4-2-3-1 and Mbappé’s pace make them the most lethal on the counter against England’s high line and Brazil’s fluid attacking shape. 
Their set-piece expertise and star-driven transitions give them an advantage in games where Argentina or England try to control possession. The ability to switch quickly between high press and compact shape allows France to adapt better than most when facing varying opposition intensity.

2. 🇸🇳Senegal🇸🇳
 (2018 & 2022 participant)
2018 (Aliou Cissé): Organized 4-3-3 or 4-2-3-1 — physical, direct, counter-attacking with wide threats.
2022 (Aliou Cissé): Compact 4-3-3 / 4-2-3-1 — high work-rate, intense pressing triggers, rapid vertical transitions.

🌟Current formation drivers🌟
Sadio Mané (125 caps, 53 goals, 32 assists – Soccer24) – The veteran winger who fuels the energetic 4-3-3 press and counters. His pace, leadership and 7.6+ ratings turn defensive recoveries into lethal transitions.

Kalidou Koulibaly (92 caps, 7 goals, 5 assists – Soccer24) – The commanding centre-back who organises the back line. His aerial dominance and 7.7+ ratings allow Senegal to press high without fear.
Strengths:
 Relentless athleticism and physical duels, dangerous counter speed, excellent team cohesion and set-piece delivery.
Drawbacks:
 Can lack creativity against deep-sitting sides.Tactical edge vs Big Four
Senegal’s energetic 4-3-3 press can disrupt Argentina’s balanced build-up and force Brazil into uncomfortable defensive spells. Their physicality and counter speed are perfectly suited to punish England’s high line and France’s occasional gaps. 
The raw athleticism and team spirit make them a dangerous physical challenger that can match any Big Four side in end-to-end games.

3.🇮🇶 Iraq🇮🇶
 (No appearances 2014-2022, last in 1986)
No World Cup data in survey period; evolution tracked via Asian qualifiers/AFC Asian Cup: Compact 4-2-3-1 or 4-3-3 — ultra-organized low/mid-block, emphasis on physical midfield and rapid counters.

🌟Current formation drivers🌟
Ali Adnan (68 caps, 9 goals, 13 assists – Soccer24) – The experienced left-back who launches rapid counters in the compact 4-2-3-1. His overlapping runs and 7.4+ ratings provide width and transition spark.

Saddam Hakeem (52 caps, 7 goals, 6 assists – Soccer24) – The midfield anchor who wins duels and organises the low/mid-block. His tenacity and 7.3+ ratings keep the shape disciplined under pressure.
Strengths:
 Ironclad defensive discipline, dangerous on the break and set-pieces.
Drawbacks:
 Significant experience gap at World Cup level.

🇮🇶 Tactical edge vs Big Four
Iraq’s compact 4-2-3-1 low block is built to absorb France and Senegal’s pressure while picking apart Norway on transitions. Their organized shape and set-piece threat can frustrate Brazil’s flair and England’s high press. 
The hard-working, disciplined approach makes Iraq the ultimate organized spoiler.

4. 🇳🇴Norway🇳🇴
 (Return after missing 2014-2022, last appearance 1998)
No recent World Cup data; evolution tracked via Nations League/Euro qualifiers: Modern 4-3-3 or 4-2-3-1 — direct, high-pressing with vertical threats and emphasis on star individuals (Haaland-led).

🌟Current formation drivers🌟
Erling Haaland (52 caps, 56 goals, 14 assists – Soccer24) – The superstar striker who finishes the aggressive 4-3-3 / 4-2-3-1 transitions. His movement, finishing and 7.8+ ratings turn vertical play into goals.
Martin Ødegaard (78 caps, 15 goals, 32 assists – Soccer24) – The creative No.10 who dictates tempo and delivers killer passes. His vision and 7.7+ ratings link midfield to attack and unlock deep defences.
Strengths:
 Explosive attacking talent, intense pressing, dangerous transitions and aerial duels.
Drawbacks:
 Limited top-level tournament rhythm.

🇳🇴 Tactical edge vs Big Four
Norway’s aggressive 4-3-3 press can trouble Senegal’s build-up and Iraq’s deep sits, but France’s superior quality will likely pin them back. Their superstar firepower and vertical threats make them dangerous in transition moments against any Big Four side that overcommits. 
The explosive athleticism and individual brilliance of Haaland and Ødegaard give Norway the potential to create chaos.

⚽️Key Group I Verdict vs Big Four⚽️
Senegal stands out as the most dangerous threat in Group I. Their ferocious athleticism and pressing intensity are perfectly suited to punish the exact weaknesses of the Big Four. 
France is the clear favorite, but one slip against the African Lions (or Norway’s firepower) could hand Group I a historic upset.
🔥⁉️What do you think, Chronicles crew?
Can Senegal or Norway spring a surprise against France? Drop your boldest predictions below! #WorldCup2026 #GroupI #TacticalEvolution #GiantKilling

Drawbacks: 
Vulnerable to ultra-compact low blocks.

🇫🇷 Tactical edge vs Big Four
France’s dynamic 4-2-3-1 and Mbappé’s pace make them the most lethal on the counter against England’s high line and Brazil’s fluid attacking shape. 
Their set-piece expertise and star-driven transitions give them an advantage in games where Argentina or England try to control possession. The ability to switch quickly between high press and compact shape allows France to adapt better than most when facing varying opposition intensity.

2. 🇸🇳Senegal🇸🇳
 (2018 & 2022 participant)
2018 (Aliou Cissé): Organized 4-3-3 or 4-2-3-1 — physical, direct, counter-attacking with wide threats.
2022 (Aliou Cissé): Compact 4-3-3 / 4-2-3-1 — high work-rate, intense pressing triggers, rapid vertical transitions.

🌟Current formation drivers🌟
Sadio Mané (125 caps, 53 goals, 32 assists – Soccer24) – The veteran winger who fuels the energetic 4-3-3 press and counters. His pace, leadership and 7.6+ ratings turn defensive recoveries into lethal transitions.

Kalidou Koulibaly (92 caps, 7 goals, 5 assists – Soccer24) – The commanding centre-back who organises the back line. His aerial dominance and 7.7+ ratings allow Senegal to press high without fear.
Strengths:
 Relentless athleticism and physical duels, dangerous counter speed, excellent team cohesion and set-piece delivery.
Drawbacks:
 Can lack creativity against deep-sitting sides.Tactical edge vs Big Four
Senegal’s energetic 4-3-3 press can disrupt Argentina’s balanced build-up and force Brazil into uncomfortable defensive spells. Their physicality and counter speed are perfectly suited to punish England’s high line and France’s occasional gaps. 
The raw athleticism and team spirit make them a dangerous physical challenger that can match any Big Four side in end-to-end games.

3.🇮🇶 Iraq🇮🇶
 (No appearances 2014-2022, last in 1986)
No World Cup data in survey period; evolution tracked via Asian qualifiers/AFC Asian Cup: Compact 4-2-3-1 or 4-3-3 — ultra-organized low/mid-block, emphasis on physical midfield and rapid counters.

🌟Current formation drivers🌟
Ali Adnan (68 caps, 9 goals, 13 assists – Soccer24) – The experienced left-back who launches rapid counters in the compact 4-2-3-1. His overlapping runs and 7.4+ ratings provide width and transition spark.

Saddam Hakeem (52 caps, 7 goals, 6 assists – Soccer24) – The midfield anchor who wins duels and organises the low/mid-block. His tenacity and 7.3+ ratings keep the shape disciplined under pressure.
Strengths:
 Ironclad defensive discipline, dangerous on the break and set-pieces.
Drawbacks:
 Significant experience gap at World Cup level.

🇮🇶 Tactical edge vs Big Four
Iraq’s compact 4-2-3-1 low block is built to absorb France and Senegal’s pressure while picking apart Norway on transitions. Their organized shape and set-piece threat can frustrate Brazil’s flair and England’s high press. 
The hard-working, disciplined approach makes Iraq the ultimate organized spoiler.

4. 🇳🇴Norway🇳🇴
 (Return after missing 2014-2022, last appearance 1998)
No recent World Cup data; evolution tracked via Nations League/Euro qualifiers: Modern 4-3-3 or 4-2-3-1 — direct, high-pressing with vertical threats and emphasis on star individuals (Haaland-led).

🌟Current formation drivers🌟
Erling Haaland (52 caps, 56 goals, 14 assists – Soccer24) – The superstar striker who finishes the aggressive 4-3-3 / 4-2-3-1 transitions. His movement, finishing and 7.8+ ratings turn vertical play into goals.
Martin Ødegaard (78 caps, 15 goals, 32 assists – Soccer24) – The creative No.10 who dictates tempo and delivers killer passes. His vision and 7.7+ ratings link midfield to attack and unlock deep defences.
Strengths:
 Explosive attacking talent, intense pressing, dangerous transitions and aerial duels.
Drawbacks:
 Limited top-level tournament rhythm.

🇳🇴 Tactical edge vs Big Four
Norway’s aggressive 4-3-3 press can trouble Senegal’s build-up and Iraq’s deep sits, but France’s superior quality will likely pin them back. Their superstar firepower and vertical threats make them dangerous in transition moments against any Big Four side that overcommits. 
The explosive athleticism and individual brilliance of Haaland and Ødegaard give Norway the potential to create chaos.

⚽️Key Group I Verdict vs Big Four⚽️
Senegal stands out as the most dangerous threat in Group I. Their ferocious athleticism and pressing intensity are perfectly suited to punish the exact weaknesses of the Big Four. 
France is the clear favorite, but one slip against the African Lions (or Norway’s firepower) could hand Group I a historic upset.

column4:
Senegal 🇸🇳 and Algeria 🇩🇿 have both pledged support to Mali 🇲🇱 following recent deadly attacks by jihadist and separatist groups.

Senegal’s President Bassirou Diomaye Faye said the Malian people can rely on his country’s active solidarity to help restore peace and stability in the Sahel.

Algeria’s President Abdelmadjid Tebboune also expressed readiness to assist if requested by Bamako, emphasizing strong ties, dialogue, and respect for Mali’s unity while opposing a military solution.

Regional leaders have warned that the instability could spread to neighboring countries.

column4:
Senegal's Président Bassirou Diomaye Faye and Ousmane Sonko and carried the hopes of young people across Africa; now they are going their separate ways, and it is getting pretty bad.

President Faye has indicated he's going to fire Sonko as his Prime Minister if he no longer trusted him, stating that if Sonko "remains prime minister, it is because he retains my confidence. When that is no longer the case, there will be a new prime minister."

Faye has now dismissed the spokesperson for the presidency, Ousseynou Ly, an early member of Pastef, to replace him with Abdoulaye Tine, a lawyer and an official of the Diomaye President coalition.

But as Ousseynou Ly exits, he pledges allegiance to Sonko, insisiting that his "commitment and loyalty to the transformation project championed by PASTEF, under the leadership of President Sonko, remain unwavering."

Ousmane Sonko is the leader of Pastef party.

column4:
On the sidelines of the 5th Istanbul International Water Forum in Istanbul, a key conversation shaping the future of global water cooperation.

A productive meeting with H.E. @cheikhtdieye , Minister of Water and Sanitation of Senegal, reaffirmed the importance of strengthening coordination ahead of the UN 2026 Water Conference co-hosted by both countries. The focus is clear: turning global dialogue into actionable outcomes that can be implemented on the ground.

With Senegal’s leadership in African Ministers’ Council on Water (AMCOW), continued collaboration reflects a shared commitment to advancing Africa’s water agenda, supporting climate resilience, and delivering tangible impact across the continent.

#IIWF2026
#Resilience
#MWRI

على هامش منتدى إسطنبول الدولي الخامس للمياه في إسطنبول… لقاء يعكس أهمية الشراكات في تشكيل مستقبل المياه عالميًا.

لقاء مثمر مع السيد شيخ تيديان دايي، وزير المياه والصرف الصحي بجمهورية السنغال، أكد على أهمية تعزيز التنسيق المشترك استعدادًا لـ مؤتمر الأمم المتحدة للمياه 2026، الذي تستضيفه مصر والسنغال، مع التركيز على تحقيق نتائج عملية قابلة للتنفيذ على أرض الواقع.

وفي ظل الدور المحوري للسنغال في مجلس وزراء المياه الأفارقة (الأمكاو)، يستمر العمل المشترك لدعم قضايا المياه والمناخ في القارة الإفريقية، وتعزيز القدرة على التكيف، وتحقيق تنمية مستدامة تعود بالنفع على الشعوب.

#وزارة_الموارد_المائية_والري

[ON AIR] Senegalese President Bassirou Diomaye Faye, has warned that the ruling party may be heading toward a potential downfall amid mounting speculation that his alliance with Prime Minister Ousmane Sonko is under strain.

The remarks come as Senegal grapples with mounting economic pressures linked to rising debt and the broader impact of the war in Iran. 

In a televised address, Faye added that Sonko would remain in his position, provided he continues to "do his job properly." 

For more on this, @Lulu_Gaboo is joined on the line by Amadou Camara Gueye, Journalist and geopolitical Analyst.

#AfricaUpdate #Senegal #OusmaneSonke 
#BassirouDiomayeFaye

column4:
Senegalese President Bassirou Diomaye Faye ​said the ruling party is on a path that could lead to its downfall ‌but that the party's leader, Prime Minister Ousmane Sonko, will remain in his post if he keeps "doing his job properly".

“If Pastef’s supporters do not change course, the party risks collapsing," Faye said on Saturday, adding ​that the party garnered broad support because Senegalese people backed its ideals rather than any individual's personal ambitions.

column4:
Senegal 🇸🇳 and Algeria 🇩🇿 have both offered support to Mali 🇲🇱 following recent deadly attacks by jihadist and separatist groups. 

Senegal’s President Bassirou Diomaye Faye said the Malian people can count on his country’s active solidarity to help restore peace and stability in the Sahel. 

Algeria’s President Abdelmadjid Tebboune stated that Algeria stands ready to assist if Bamako requests it, stressing strong ties, dialogue, and respect for Mali’s unity while opposing a military solution. 

Regional leaders warn the instability could spread to neighboring countries.

column4:
Senegal's President Bassirou Diomaye Faye has publicly warned that the ruling Pastef party could be heading toward collapse—remarks that are fueling speculation of growing tensions with Prime Minister Ousmane Sonko .

Speaking on state television, Faye cautioned that “if Pastef's supporters do not change course, the party risks collapsing,” stressing that its rise to power was driven by public belief in its ideals—not “individual ambitions.”

In a pointed reminder of presidential authority, he added: “As long as Sonko remains prime minister, it is because he is doing his job properly and I am satisfied… However, the day I am no longer satisfied, I will put Senegal's interests first.”

The comments come against the backdrop of mounting economic strain, including rising debt and external pressures, as well as lingering questions about cohesion within the ruling alliance.

Sonko—once the face of the opposition—was barred from contesting the 2024 election and instead backed Faye, who went on to win and later appointed him prime minister. But cracks have since begun to show. In March, Sonko warned he could pull his party out of government and return to opposition if Faye deviates from Pastef's political vision.

With both leaders now issuing public signals, what began as a strategic partnership is increasingly being tested—raising a critical question: is this internal recalibration, or the early signs of a deeper political rupture? #Senegal #MMINews

column35:
Senegal's President Bassirou Diomaye Faye has publicly warned that the ruling Pastef party could be heading toward collapse—remarks that are fueling speculation of growing tensions with Prime Minister Ousmane Sonko .

Speaking on state television, Faye cautioned that “if Pastef's supporters do not change course, the party risks collapsing,” stressing that its rise to power was driven by public belief in its ideals—not “individual ambitions.”

In a pointed reminder of presidential authority, he added: “As long as Sonko remains prime minister, it is because he is doing his job properly and I am satisfied… However, the day I am no longer satisfied, I will put Senegal's interests first.”

The comments come against the backdrop of mounting economic strain, including rising debt and external pressures, as well as lingering questions about cohesion within the ruling alliance.

Sonko—once the face of the opposition—was barred from contesting the 2024 election and instead backed Faye, who went on to win and later appointed him prime minister. But cracks have since begun to show. In March, Sonko warned he could pull his party out of government and return to opposition if Faye deviates from Pastef's political vision.

With both leaders now issuing public signals, what began as a strategic partnership is increasingly being tested—raising a critical question: is this internal recalibration, or the early signs of a deeper political rupture? #Senegal #MMINews

column4:
Controversial referee Jean-Jacques Ndala has been appointed to officiate the first leg of the CAF Champions League final between Mamelodi Sundowns and FAR Rabat in Pretoria.

Ndala has recently faced heavy criticism following questionable decisions in an AFCON clash between Morocco and Senegal, which even saw Senegal players walk off in protest. Despite that, he brings experience, having handled multiple major finals — including one involving Sundowns.

The second leg in Morocco will be officiated by Somali referee Omar Artan.

With so much at stake, is this a risky call by CAF or the right choice based on experience?

column4:
🔴 Diomaye Faye discusses relations with Ousmane Sonko. 

“I am the President, and he is the Prime Minister. If he does not meet my expectations, I will appoint a different Prime Minister. As long as he remains Prime Minister, it signifies that he retains my confidence. If he no longer enjoys that confidence, we will have a different Prime Minister. As long as he is still here, it means he is performing his duties properly. If I cease to be satisfied, I will put Senegal first.

I built PASTEF with my own hands, in all humility, in concert with fellow patriots. Ousmane Sonko has never subscribed to the cult of personality surrounding the 'Guide' of a revolution. PASTEF has embarked on a trajectory that risks leading to its undoing. Senegal has no need for a 'Messiah' or a 'Hero'...
There is no pact between Ousmane and me. To have a pact with him would be a sign of disrespect.”  

- President Diomaye Faye

May 3 marks World Press Freedom Day, established in 1993 to honor Africa’s Windhoek Declaration.

Here is what some African leaders said to honor this day:

🔹 Netumbo Nandi-Ndaitwah, President of Namibia, said rising tensions and misinformation make a free press vital for truth and public trust.

🔹 Zhemu Soda, Minister of Information, Zimbabwe, described press freedom as a bridge to connect communities and build a shared national story.

🔹 Patrick Muyaya, DRC Government Spokesperson, reaffirmed his country’s commitment to a freer, economically viable press that fights disinformation.

🔹 Bassirou Diomaye Faye, President of Senegal, stated that his country maintains a free press even when it challenges those in power, describing this as true democracy.

▶️ Video shows vibrant street celebrations of World Press Freedom Day in Zambia 👇

Senegal’s President Bassirou Diomaye Faye has publicly reaffirmed his authority over Prime Minister Ousmane Sonko.

“I’m the President, and Sonko is Prime Minister. If he no longer satisfies me, I’ll fire him and appoint another PM,” Faye said.

The remarks highlight underlying tensions within the country’s executive leadership and reinforce the constitutional powers of the presidency.

I found an economic research paper I wrote in college on Senegal, for an "Africa's Role in World Affairs" class - we had to take a position on investing in an African country, and explain the systems.   It was my first year in college and there are a lot of errors.  

Senegal

A View of African Democracy

Africa in World Affairs

Description of Current Status of the Country and Political Regime:

Since declaring its independence from France in 1960, Senegal has developed in to a small but seemingly functional democracy, consisting of executive, judicial, and legislative branches of government. The current status of this democratic system of government consists of a president that is elected by direct universal suffrage, all genders and races eighteen years of age or older, every seven years. The president then appoints the Prime Minister for seven a year term. The legislative branch of the Senegal Government is the National Assembly, which consists of one hundred twenty seats, the elected officials serve 5 year terms, and are granted their seat in the National assembly thru direct universal suffrage. Separate elections are held for each branch of government. As a significant sign of regime stability, Senegal has a history of holding elections on time, and with a minimal amount of scandal.  Internal conflict is sporadic. The Casamance region of southern Senegal contains separatists, which wage battle with the Senegalese Military, over a several decade old dispute regarding the independence of the separatists whom straddle the border of Guinea-Bissau. Aside from the periodic violent clashes between the military and the separatists, the Republic of Senegal is a quasi-stable democracy, especially in contrast to various other African countries.

Political Culture:
1. Briefly describe the Authority Orientation: Allegiant, Alienated, Apathetic, or Mixed.

The behavior and actions taken by the president and prime minister appear to be somewhat allegiant. This country of Senegal is a functioning democracy within Africa. I found an economic research paper I wrote in college on Senegal, for an "Africa's Role in World Affairs" class - we had to take a position on investing in an African country, and explain the systems. It was my first year in college and there are a lot of errors. 

Senegal

A View of African Democracy

Africa in World Affairs

Description of Current Status of the Country and Political Regime:

Since declaring its independence from France in 1960, Senegal has developed in to a small but seemingly functional democracy, consisting of executive, judicial, and legislative branches of government. The current status of this democratic system of government consists of a president that is elected by direct universal suffrage, all genders and races eighteen years of age or older, every seven years. The president then appoints the Prime Minister for seven a year term. The legislative branch of the Senegal Government is the National Assembly, which consists of one hundred twenty seats, the elected officials serve 5 year terms, and are granted their seat in the National assembly thru direct universal suffrage. Separate elections are held for each branch of government. As a significant sign of regime stability, Senegal has a history of holding elections on time, and with a minimal amount of scandal. Internal conflict is sporadic. The Casamance region of southern Senegal contains separatists, which wage battle with the Senegalese Military, over a several decade old dispute regarding the independence of the separatists whom straddle the border of Guinea-Bissau. Aside from the periodic violent clashes between the military and the separatists, the Republic of Senegal is a quasi-stable democracy, especially in contrast to various other African countries.

Political Culture:
1. Briefly describe the Authority Orientation: Allegiant, Alienated, Apathetic, or Mixed.

The behavior and actions taken by the president and prime minister appear to be somewhat allegiant. This country of Senegal is a functioning democracy within Africa. A new ILO report shines a light on a phenomenon that remains largely invisible across the continent; under-reported, poorly documented and rooted in deep structural inequalities. More than half of workers in Côte d'Ivoire and Senegal have experienced violence or harassment in the workplace, while in Burkina Faso, the figure is nearly one in two.

Victims report deteriorating mental health, falling productivity and rising absenteeism, causing ripple effects across entire economies.

Is workplace violence in Africa a problem of unchecked individuals, or is it a cultural issue?

had the privilege of presenting at the Regional Leadership Meeting in Senegal, engaging with UNFPA Representatives in 22 countries across the region on issues that sit at the heart of our mandate and impact.

In my remarks, I emphasized that integrity is not an abstract principle—it is a daily commitment that underpins credibility, trust, and the effectiveness of our work. It is essential that we continue to foster a culture where ethical conduct is expected, supported, and upheld at all levels.

I also highlighted the critical role of the Office of Audit and Investigation Services (OAIS)—not only as an independent assurance function, but as a trusted advisor to management. By providing insight, foresight, and practical recommendations, OAIS contributes to strengthening governance, risk management, and internal controls across the organization.

Equally important is our collective responsibility to safeguard UNFPA resources. Every decision we make must reflect stewardship, accountability, and a clear focus on ensuring that resources reach those who need them most.

Ultimately, protecting resources is inseparable from protecting the communities we serve—especially women and girls. Upholding integrity and accountability is how we ensure that our mission translates into real, meaningful impact on the ground.

Thank you to colleagues for the open dialogue and continued commitment to these shared values.

column4:
History is too rich for us to be intimidated by detention.

Again and again, men who were detained, persecuted, imprisoned, silenced, and politically targeted eventually rose stronger, bigger, and more accepted by the people than those who tried to stop them. Global political history is full of such examples.  

When Nelson Mandela was unjustly locked away for 27 years, the apartheid regime thought prison would bury his voice, but prison only made him the moral owner of South Africa.

When Olusegun Obasanjo was unfairly sent to prison under Abacha, many thought his political life had ended, yet shortly after release he emerged President of Nigeria.

When Bassirou Diomaye Faye was detained in Senegal, the establishment believed they had neutralized him, but the prison gate became the gate of the presidential villa.

When José Mujica spent 14 years in military imprisonment, his captors never imagined he would later become the beloved president of Uruguay.  

When Lech Wałęsa was repeatedly detained by communist authorities, detention turned him into the symbol of democratic Poland.

When Václav Havel was jailed as a dissident writer, the state thought they were silencing a critic, but they were preparing a president.

When Robert Mugabe spent years in Rhodesian prison, it only deepened his revolutionary legitimacy before independence.

When Juan Perón was detained by rivals, the public outrage over his arrest made him more popular than before and he went on to win power.

When Lula da Silva was imprisoned amid political controversy, many believed he was finished, yet he returned to reclaim Brazil’s highest office.

When Kwame Nkrumah won elections from prison, colonial authorities learned the hard way that confinement cannot defeat conviction.  

So why should anyone think that a detention, summons, harassment, or coordinated political persecution is enough to erase Mallam Nasir El-Rufai?

Mallam Nasir El-Rufai’s present ordeal should not frighten anyone who genuinely believes that Nigeria needs bold, competent, and intellectually courageous leadership, infact I believe it should fuel our desire for it.

Senegal’s parliament has passed a controversial electoral reform ahead of the 2029 presidential election.

The bill, approved by 128 votes to 11, seeks to change eligibility rules tied to criminal convictions — a move the opposition says could reshape who can run for office.

“This law is for one person only,” said opposition leader Aissata Tall Sall.

The government insists the reform is a democratic update to expand participation. The bill now awaits presidential approval or possible legal challenge.

#Senegal #AfricaNews #Politics #ElectionReform #WestAfrica

column4:
Facing Diminishing Presence in Africa, Macron Looks to Kenya for Reconfiguration

As France’s influence across Africa continues to wane, French President Emmanuel Macron heads to Kenya for the first Africa Forward summit with English-speaking African nations, writes Thuto Mashaba. The one-time colonial power, which once maintained permanent military footholds in West and Central Africa, has now been largely rejected across the continent – especially in the Sahel.

“France’s pretty prose of ‘mutual cooperation’ and ‘shared development’ no longer hides its ruthless ambition to partner with Africa solely for resource extraction and geopolitical presence,” says political analyst Kim Heller. “There is little respect for Africa’s sovereignty.”

The numbers tell a stark story. France now accounts for just 1.9% of Africa’s foreign trade, and its military footprint has collapsed. In recent years, France has lost bases in Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger, Chad, Ivory Coast, and Senegal. Senegal’s new president, Bassirou Diomaye Faye, has not only demanded a French withdrawal but also an apology for colonial-era atrocities, including the 1944 massacre of African troops who fought for France in World War II.

As former colonies turn away, France has been forced to look elsewhere.
