column35:
Facing Diminishing Presence in Africa, Macron Looks to Kenya for Reconfiguration

As France’s influence across Africa continues to wane, French President Emmanuel Macron heads to Kenya for the first Africa Forward summit with English-speaking African nations, writes Thuto Mashaba. The one-time colonial power, which once maintained permanent military footholds in West and Central Africa, has now been largely rejected across the continent – especially in the Sahel.

“France’s pretty prose of ‘mutual cooperation’ and ‘shared development’ no longer hides its ruthless ambition to partner with Africa solely for resource extraction and geopolitical presence,” says political analyst Kim Heller. “There is little respect for Africa’s sovereignty.”

The numbers tell a stark story. France now accounts for just 1.9% of Africa’s foreign trade, and its military footprint has collapsed. In recent years, France has lost bases in Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger, Chad, Ivory Coast, and Senegal. Senegal’s new president, Bassirou Diomaye Faye, has not only demanded a French withdrawal but also an apology for colonial-era atrocities, including the 1944 massacre of African troops who fought for France in World War II.

As former colonies turn away, France has been forced to look elsewhere. Its top two trading partners in sub-Saharan Africa are now Nigeria and South Africa both former British colonies that have never hosted a French military base. In a telling diplomatic shift, Macron withdrew South Africa’s invitation to a G7 meeting in favour of Kenya, prompting President Cyril Ramaphosa to likely skip the upcoming Nairobi summit.

French Ambassador Alexis Lamek has admitted that the summit must address restitution and reparations, issues central to the African Union’s agenda. Security analyst Emmanuel Dupuy puts it bluntly: “France is very poorly equipped perhaps even completely disarmed to be credible in this region.”

Macron insists on fighting a “battle of narratives” against rising anti-French sentiment, but critics argue Paris is merely reacting to forces beyond its control. As Heller concludes: “If France cannot acquiesce to equitability and respect for African sovereignty, it should not be a partner of choice.”

By Thuto Mashaba

column4:
Senegal’s parliament has approved a reform allowing Prime Minister Ousmane Sonko to run in the 2029 presidential election.

Sonko, a popular figure among young voters, was barred from the 2024 race over a defamation conviction. His ally Bassirou Diomaye Faye ran in his place and won in the first round.

The new law amends the electoral code, removing defamation convictions as grounds for ineligibility.

According to The Times, FIFA is set to introduce red cards at the 2026 FIFA World Cup for players who cover their mouths when confronting opponents and for players who leave the pitch in protest of a referee’s decision 🟥

ASA Situation Report™ Dakar Forum 2026 (20–21 Apr, Diamniadio) marked a decade of Sahel rupture: G5 Sahel collapse, Mali–Burkina–Niger exit from ECOWAS, jihadist spillover toward Gulf of Guinea states, and a hardening “sovereignty-first” security agenda. Leaders urged an African security doctrine as external competition (Russia/Türkiye/UAE/China/West) intensifies, critical minerals reshape leverage, and cyber threats grow – while terrorism deaths in the Sahel are cited as ~10x higher than 2007.

Mali To Establish Direct Trade Route With Senegal

The government of Mali has reached a landmark agreement with Senegal to build an $800 million sea corridor connecting the two nations. This project is aimed at securing access for the landlocked Mali to the Atlantic Ocean, thus beefing up its traditional trade routes and boosting its economy, and comes amid rising tensions in the Sahel.

As a member of the Alliance of Sahel States (AES), Mali has faced relentless attacks by Western-backed terrorists, economic isolation and sovereignty violations by Western-aligned African states, and endless slander from Western and Western-aligned media. Despite these externally-imposed challenges, the country and its fellow AES members, Niger and Burkina Faso, have continued to record economic and political wins.

All 3 AES members have pointed to former colonizer France as a key sponsor of terror in the region – a claim which has been corroborated by their international allies – and France itself has made no bones about its intentions to revive its dwindling influence in Africa, and in so doing, shore up its presently crumbling economy.

On April 25, 2026, the Malian Armed Forces (FAMa) swiftly neutralized a series of tellingly well-coordinated terror attacks across the country, marking yet another victory for the sovereign Mali, and the wider AES, against the enemies of Africa.

column4:
𝗕𝗹𝘂𝗲 𝗖𝗹𝗼𝘂𝗱 𝗦𝗼𝗳𝘁𝗲𝗰𝗵 𝗦𝗼𝗹𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀 𝗟𝘁𝗱. 𝗦𝗶𝗴𝗻𝘀 𝗦𝘁𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗴𝗶𝗰 𝗠𝗼𝗨 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝗔𝗳𝗿𝗶𝗰𝗮𝗻 𝗘𝘅𝗽𝗮𝗻𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻 🤝:

Blue Cloud Softech Solutions Limited (BCSSL) has entered into a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) with GCIB for Africa Ltd. (Global Council for Investment and Business for Africa) to foster collaboration on strategic initiatives and technology-driven socio-economic projects across Senegal and other African nations. This marks a significant strategic move for BCSSL into the burgeoning African market. 🌍

𝗞𝗲𝘆 𝗢𝗯𝗷𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲𝘀 𝗼𝗳 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗠𝗼𝗨:

- Establish a non-exclusive framework for collaboration.
- Explore the formation of Joint Ventures (JVs) or Special Purpose Vehicles (SPVs) for project execution.
- Engage with the Government of Senegal & private stakeholders for high-impact infrastructure & services.
- Contemplate sector-specific SPVs/JVs in renewable energy, IT, infrastructure, education, & healthcare.

𝗕𝗖𝗦𝗦𝗟'𝘀 𝗥𝗼𝗹𝗲:

- Provide technology solutions, platforms, domain expertise, & technical manpower.
- Lead execution in digital, healthcare, cybersecurity, & AI-driven initiatives.
- Facilitate technical partnerships & sourcing of necessary hardware/equipment.
- Undertake project management, implementation, & maintenance.

𝗚𝗖𝗜𝗕 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝗔𝗳𝗿𝗶𝗰𝗮 𝗟𝘁𝗱.'𝘀 𝗥𝗼𝗹𝗲:

- Facilitate government relationships & approvals.
- Provide institutional access & stakeholder alignment.
- Assist with land allocation & local regulatory navigation.
- Support investor outreach & local market assessment.

𝗦𝘁𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗴𝗶𝗰 𝗦𝗶𝗴𝗻𝗶𝗳𝗶𝗰𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗲 & 𝗠𝗮𝗿𝗸𝗲𝘁 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗲𝘅𝘁:

This partnership aligns with the trend of Indian tech firms seeking growth in emerging African digital economies. Senegal is highlighted as a strategic gateway due to its stable economy, government focus on digital growth (Plan Sénégal Emergent), & robust digital infrastructure. 🚀

- 𝗧𝗲𝗰𝗵𝗻𝗼𝗹𝗼𝗴𝘆 𝗣𝘂𝘀𝗵: BCSSL aims to export its AI, Blockchain, & HealthTech solutions.
- 𝗘𝗰𝗼𝗻𝗼𝗺𝗶𝗰 𝗣𝘂𝗹𝗹: Driven by Senegal's demand for digitized services & industrial automation.
- 𝗦𝘁𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗴𝗶𝗰 𝗚𝗼𝗮𝗹: Establishing Senegal as a regional tech hub.

𝗙𝗶𝗻𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗶𝗮𝗹 𝗢𝘂𝘁𝗹𝗼𝗼𝗸:

The MoU is non-binding, & the financial impact is not quantifiable at this stage. Any financial commitments will arise from future definitive agreements. 💰

Blue Cloud Softech Solutions Limited (BCSSL) has entered into a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) with GCIB for Africa Ltd. (Global Council for Investment and Business for Africa) to foster collaboration on strategic initiatives and technology-driven socio-economic projects across Senegal and other African nations. This marks a significant strategic move for BCSSL into the burgeoning African market. 🌍

- Establish a non-exclusive framework for collaboration.
- Explore the formation of Joint Ventures (JVs) or Special Purpose Vehicles (SPVs) for project execution.
- Engage with the Government of Senegal & private stakeholders for high-impact infrastructure & services.
- Contemplate sector-specific SPVs/JVs in renewable energy, IT, infrastructure, education, & healthcare.

𝗕𝗖𝗦𝗦𝗟'𝘀 𝗥𝗼𝗹𝗲:

- Provide technology solutions, platforms, domain expertise, & technical manpower.
- Lead execution in digital, healthcare, cybersecurity, & AI-driven initiatives.
- Facilitate technical partnerships & sourcing of necessary hardware/equipment.
- Undertake project management, implementation, & maintenance.

- Facilitate government relationships & approvals.
- Provide institutional access & stakeholder alignment.
- Assist with land allocation & local regulatory navigation.
- Support investor outreach & local market assessment.

𝗦𝘁𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗴𝗶𝗰 𝗦𝗶𝗴𝗻𝗶𝗳𝗶𝗰𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗲 & 𝗠𝗮𝗿𝗸𝗲𝘁 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗲𝘅𝘁:

This partnership aligns with the trend of Indian tech firms seeking growth in emerging African digital economies. Senegal is highlighted as a strategic gateway due to its stable economy, government focus on digital growth (Plan Sénégal Emergent), & robust digital infrastructure. 🚀

- 𝗧𝗲𝗰𝗵𝗻𝗼𝗹𝗼𝗴𝘆 𝗣𝘂𝘀𝗵: BCSSL aims to export its AI, Blockchain, & HealthTech solutions.
- 𝗘𝗰𝗼𝗻𝗼𝗺𝗶𝗰 𝗣𝘂𝗹𝗹: Driven by Senegal's demand for digitized services & industrial automation.

column4:
Is Russia losing influence in Africa?

Mali has become a test of Russia’s presence in Africa. The events of April 25-27, 2026 - coordinated attacks across the country, the death of defense minister Sadio Camara (one of the architects of Mali’s alliance with Moscow), fighting for Kidal, and the withdrawal of Russia’s Africa Corps from the city - have shattered a key Russian narrative: "security in exchange for loyalty."

The Africa Corps is often described as a replacement for Wagner. But the reality is more complex. After the death of Yevgeny Prigozhin, the Kremlin did not simply rebrand Wagner. It began dismantling Prigozhin’s empire into separate components: military, political, media, economic, and intelligence. What had previously functioned as a semi-private system was gradually integrated into Russia’s state structures.

▪️ The first layer is the military component.
The Africa Corps, linked to Russia’s defense ministry, has taken over Wagner’s functions in Mali, Libya, Burkina Faso, and Niger. In the Central African Republic, the process has been more complex: Wagner’s existing infrastructure retained autonomy for longer, and local authorities preferred established personal channels. As a result, the state takeover proceeded more slowly.

▪️ The second layer is intelligence and influence.
Investigations by Forbidden Stories, Dossier Center, iStories, openDemocracy, and All Eyes on Wagner found that Wagner’s political and information network - known as Africa Politology or "the Company" - came under the oversight of Russia’s foreign intelligence service after Prigozhin’s death. This network includes political consultants, media intermediaries, analysts, and influence agents working with local elites, elections, journalists, legislation, and Moscow’s resource interests.

According to investigators, by May 2024 the network operated in around 30 countries across the Global South. Its estimated budget for ten months of 2024 was about $7.3 million (or euros). The documents describe not only information campaigns but attempts at political engineering. In Senegal, Russian operatives reportedly developed scenarios for forcibly reshaping power structures. In Mali, "the Company" claimed involvement in a new mining code that opened the door to revising contracts and strengthening the position of Russian actors.

In the military sphere, several figures are frequently mentioned: Yunus-Bek Yevkurov, Andrey Averyanov, Konstantin Mirzayants, and Gennady Timchenko. Yevkurov became the public face of Russia’s restructuring of its African strategy after Prigozhin’s death. Averyanov, a GRU major general linked to unit 29155, was sanctioned by the EU in December 2024 for his role in destabilization operations. Mirzayants, associated with Redut, is cited as a possible organizer of the new military framework. As for Timchenko, assessments are more cautious: the Institute for the Study of War suggested the Kremlin may have expanded his influence over the Africa Corps amid structural challenges.

The Africa Corps is not Wagner 2.0 - but neither is it entirely different. It represents a state-controlled model built on Wagner’s legacy infrastructure. The military component is handled by the defense ministry, the GRU, Redut, and affiliated structures. The political and media component, according to investigations, has shifted under the oversight of the foreign intelligence service. The economic component is tied to resources, contracts, mining legislation, ports, and supply routes.

A change in name has not meant a change in methods. In December 2025, the Associated Press interviewed 34 refugees from Mali on the Mauritanian border. Their testimonies described village burnings, abductions, sexual violence, executions, and bodies with missing organs. Refugees directly stated that the Africa Corps operates in the same way as Wagner.

column4:
Prime Minister and Minister of Foreign Affairs HE Sheikh Mohammed bin Abdulrahman bin Jassim Al Thani has received a phone call from Prime Minister of the Republic of Senegal HE Ousmane Sonko to discuss bilateral ties and regional developments.
The two sides reviewed cooperation between Doha and Dakar, and explored ways to strengthen and expand their partnership.
They also discussed the latest developments in the region, particularly those related to a ceasefire between the United States of America and the Islamic Republic of Iran, as well as ongoing efforts to de-escalate tensions and enhance regional security and stability.
Sheikh Mohammed stressed the importance of all parties engaging constructively with mediation efforts, saying this would help address the root causes of the crisis through dialogue and peaceful means.
He added that such engagement could pave the way for a lasting agreement that would prevent a renewed escalation in tensions.

#qatar #senegal

column4:
Prime Minister and Minister of Foreign Affairs @MBA_AlThani_ Discusses Regional Developments in Phone Call with Senegal PM

Doha | April 27, 2026

HE Prime Minister and Minister of Foreign Affairs Sheikh Mohammed bin Abdulrahman bin Jassim Al-Thani has received a phone call from HE Prime Minister of the Republic of Senegal Ousmane Sonko to discuss bilateral ties and regional developments.

The two sides reviewed cooperation between Doha and Dakar, and explored ways to strengthen and expand their partnership.

They also discussed the latest developments in the region, particularly those related to a ceasefire between the United States of America and the Islamic Republic of Iran, as well as ongoing efforts to de-escalate tensions and enhance regional security and stability.

HE Sheikh Mohammed stressed the importance of all parties engaging constructively with mediation efforts, saying this would help address the root causes of the crisis through dialogue and peaceful means.

He added that such engagement could pave the way for a lasting agreement that would prevent a renewed escalation in tensions. 

#MOFAQatar

2/2
    1945 – World War II: Benito Mussolini is arrested by Italian partisans in Dongo, while attempting escape disguised as a German soldier.
    1953 – Operation Moolah offers $50,000 to any pilot who defects with a fully mission-capable Mikoyan-Gurevich MiG-15 to South Korea. The first pilot was to receive $100,000.
    1959 – The Soviet Union and Egypt sign an agreement to build the Aswan High Dam.
    1967 – Expo 67 officially opens in Montreal, Quebec, Canada with a large opening ceremony broadcast around the world. It opens to the public the next day.
    1974 – 109 people are killed in a plane crash near Pulkovo Airport.
    1976 – Thirty-seven people are killed when American Airlines Flight 625 crashes at Cyril E. King Airport in Saint Thomas, U.S. Virgin Islands.
    1978 – John Ehrlichman, a former aide to U.S. President Richard Nixon, is released from the Federal Correctional Institution, Safford, Arizona, after serving 18 months for Watergate-related crimes.
    1978 – The Saur Revolution begins in Afghanistan, ending the following morning with the murder of Afghan President Mohammed Daoud Khan and the establishment of the Democratic Republic of Afghanistan.
    1978 – Willow Island disaster: In the deadliest construction accident in United States history, 51 construction workers are killed when a cooling tower under construction collapses at the Pleasants Power Station in Willow Island, West Virginia.
    1986 – The city of Pripyat and surrounding areas are evacuated due to the Chernobyl disaster.
    1987 – The U.S. Department of Justice bars Austrian President Kurt Waldheim (and his wife, Elisabeth, who had also been a Nazi) from entering the US, charging that he had aided in the deportations and executions of thousands of Jews and others as a German Army officer during World War II.
    1989 – The April 27 demonstrations, student-led protests responding to the April 26 Editorial, during the Tiananmen Square protests of 1989.
    1992 – The Federal Republic of Yugoslavia, comprising Serbia and Montenegro, is proclaimed.
    1992 – Betty Boothroyd becomes the first woman to be elected Speaker of the British House of Commons in its 700-year history.
    1992 – The Russian Federation and 12 other former Soviet republics become members of the International Monetary Fund and the World Bank.
    1993 – For the first time since 1949, there was a meeting between representatives of China and Taiwan.
    1993 – Most of the Zambia national football team lose their lives in a plane crash off Libreville, Gabon en route to Dakar, Senegal to play a 1994 FIFA World Cup qualifying match against Senegal.
    1994 – South African general election: The first democratic general election in South Africa, in which black citizens could vote. The Interim Constitution comes into force.
    2014 – Unique in the history of Christianity, two popes were canonized simultaneously, John XXIII and John Paul II.
    2005 – Airbus A380 aircraft has its maiden test flight.
    2006 – Construction begins on the Freedom Tower (later renamed One World Trade Center) in New York City.
    2007 – Estonian authorities remove the Bronze Soldier, a Soviet Red Army war memorial in Tallinn, amid political controversy with Russia.
    2007 – Israeli archaeologists discover the tomb of Herod the Great south of Jerusalem.
    2011 – The 2011 Super Outbreak devastates parts of the Southeastern United States, especially the states of Alabama, Mississippi, Georgia, and Tennessee. Two hundred five tornadoes touched down on April 27 alone, killing more than 300 and injuring hundreds more.
    2012 – A series of terrorist attacks occurred in the center of Dnipropetrovsk (Ukraine).
    2014 – Declaration of independence of the Luhansk People's Republic.
    2018 – The Panmunjom Declaration is signed between North and South Korea, officially declaring their intentions to end the Korean conflict.

🇸🇳The Government of the Republic of Senegal is following with grave concern the evolving security situation in Mali, marked by coordinated attacks perpetrated on April 25, 2026 against several military positions and urban areas. 

Senegal condemns in the strongest possible terms these acts of violence, attributed to armed terrorist groups, which seriously endanger civilian populations and undermine the stability of Mali and the entire region.

He expresses his full solidarity with the Government of Mali and the Malian people, as well as his heartfelt condolences to the families of the victims. He also affirms his support for the Malian defense and security forces engaged in the fight against terrorism.

column4:
Pan-African activist Kemi Seba has been detained in South Africa, days after his arrest in Pretoria while allegedly attempting to flee to Europe via Zimbabwe. Authorities say the 45-year-old is wanted in Benin for “inciting rebellion” linked to a failed coup attempt in December 2025.

South African officials confirmed Seba and his 18-year-old son were arrested for breaching immigration laws, including overstaying a visa. Police also described him as a “wanted fugitive” in both France and Benin, adding that an extradition process is now under way, though it remains unclear which country will formally secure his transfer.

Benin’s government has already signalled its intention to request extradition. Prosecutors accuse Seba of backing the attempted overthrow of President Patrice Talon and have issued international arrest warrants, alongside allegations of money laundering. Seba has not publicly responded to the charges.

Born in Strasbourg, France, to Beninese parents, Seba—whose real name is Stellio Gilles Robert Capo Chichi—has built a reputation as a radical anti-colonial voice. He is widely known for his fierce opposition to French influence in Africa, criticism of the CFA franc, and support for military-led governments in parts of West Africa.

His activism has repeatedly brought him into conflict with authorities across several countries. In France, he was convicted on multiple occasions for inciting racial hatred and antisemitism, and groups he founded were banned. He later relocated to Senegal and expanded his influence through the organisation Pan-Africanist Emergency, which campaigns against what it describes as neocolonial control.

Seba gained wider prominence in 2017 after publicly burning a CFA franc note in Senegal, a symbolic protest against the currency used by several former French colonies. His stance has resonated with segments of younger West Africans increasingly critical of France’s role in the region.

In recent years, he has also been linked politically to Sahelian military regimes, including in Niger, where he was appointed adviser to junta leader Abdourahmane Tchiani. French officials have previously accused him of promoting pro-Russian narratives, allegations his supporters reject.

His latest arrest comes amid growing geopolitical tensions in West Africa, where anti-French sentiment and shifting alliances toward Russia have reshaped regional dynamics.

Seba is due to appear in court for a bail hearing on April 29. Meanwhile, his organisation has dismissed the accusations against him as politically motivated, urging supporters to remain calm while legal proceedings unfold.

#MMINews

THE SILENCED MILLIONS: WHY NIGERIA’S 16 MILLION DIASPORA CITIZENS MUST HAVE THE RIGHT TO VOTE AND RUN FOR OFFICE
By Kio Amachree | Stockholm, Sweden

Nigeria just passed a new Electoral Act. President Bola Tinubu signed it into law in February 2026. The National Assembly debated it at length. Commentators praised its technological provisions — electronic accreditation, mandatory result transmission, the abolition of indirect primaries. They called it historic.
It is not historic. It is incomplete. And its incompleteness is a scandal.

Forty African nations have already adopted provisions allowing their diaspora citizens to vote from abroad. Nigeria has not. ￼ In a continent where Nigeria positions itself as the giant, where Nigerian leaders travel the world demanding respect at the G20, at the UN, at Davos — Nigeria cannot find the political will to do what Senegal, Ghana, Mozambique, and Rwanda have already done.

I was born in Lagos when Nigeria still had a colonial Governor-General. I served my National Youth Service. I have worked in Nigeria, invested in Nigeria, promoted Nigeria on every stage available to me across five decades. Today I live in Stockholm, Sweden — a country of ten million people. And I have no right to vote in the country of my birth. I have no right to stand for office. I am, in the eyes of Nigeria’s electoral architecture, a ghost.

That is not democracy. That is a disgrace.
There are sixteen million Nigerians living outside Nigeria. Let that number sit with you. Sixteen million. The entire population of Sweden — one of the most prosperous and stable nations on earth — is ten million. The Nigerian diaspora exceeds it by sixty percent. We are not a footnote. We are not an afterthought. We are a nation within a nation, and we are being deliberately excluded from the democracy we finance.

Diaspora remittances represent approximately 6.1 percent of Nigeria’s national GDP. ￼ We send home fifty-four billion dollars annually — more than the combined federal allocations to health, education, and infrastructure in most budget cycles. We are, in economic terms, one of Nigeria’s most productive sectors. Yet we are denied the most elementary right of citizenship: the ballot.

The Speaker of the Nigerian House of Representatives publicly advocated for a diaspora voting bill in late 2025, emphasizing that Nigerians abroad should have the opportunity to participate in national elections. ￼ INEC itself has stated publicly that Nigerians in the diaspora should have the right to vote. The INEC Chairman confirmed that the Commission believes Nigerians abroad should vote, citing their citizenship, their financial contributions, their numbers, and the fact that diaspora voting reflects global best practice. ￼ Yet the new Electoral Act arrived and departed without a single provision granting us that right.

Political will in Nigeria has a habit of evaporating between statement and statute.
The standard objection is logistical. Cost. Security. The complexity of overseas registration. These arguments are recycled decade after decade by a political class that understands exactly what diaspora enfranchisement would mean: accountability. A diaspora voter does not live under fuel scarcity. He cannot be bought with a bag of rice and five hundred naira on election morning. She cannot be intimidated by a political thug at a polling unit. We vote with clarity because we live with electricity, with functional institutions, with the lived experience of what governance can look like when it is not captured by criminals.

They say we are out of touch. The internet has destroyed that argument permanently. I know more about what is happening in Abuja, in Port Harcourt, in Owerri on any given morning than most senators sitting in the chamber. We are not distant. We are detached — against our will — from the process.

Any constitutional amendment enabling diaspora voting requires passage through both chambers of the National Assembly, ratification by two-thirds of state assemblies, and presidential assent. ￼ The political class has used this complexity as a permanent excuse for inaction. But complexity is not impossibility. It is a choice — and Nigeria keeps choosing exclusion.
The man whose fabricated biography has never been fully examined by any electoral tribunal sits in Aso Rock. He did not complete his National Youth Service. Questions about his identity, his academic records, his legal history in the United States remain open and unresolved. And yet I — a man whose father was Nigeria’s first Solicitor-General, whose grandfather participated in the 1958 Constitutional Conference that gave Nigeria its independence date, whose family’s name is written into the founding of this nation — cannot cast a single vote.

Something is profoundly wrong with that sentence.
The 2027 elections are approaching. The window for constitutional amendment is narrow but not closed. The National Assembly must act — not with press conferences, not with committee visits to INEC, not with diaspora town halls that produce memoranda filed and forgotten. It must act with legislation. It must amend the relevant sections of the 1999 Constitution. It must grant the sixteen million full citizenship rights — the right to register, the right to vote, and the right to contest for office.

We wave the flag. We bear the passport. We carry Nigeria with us everywhere we go — in our names, our music, our advocacy, our cuisine, our children. We remit the billions that keep the economy from complete collapse. We represent this nation on foreign soil every single day without salary, without portfolio, and without recognition.
The least Nigeria owes us is a vote.

Fifty-four billion dollars buys representation. Or it should. If the National Assembly cannot understand that equation, then perhaps the diaspora should begin to make the calculation in the other direction — and let Nigeria feel what sixteen million invisible citizens actually mean to its survival.

The time for half-measures is over. Enfranchise the diaspora. Open the candidacy. Do it before 2027 — or answer, permanently, why you chose not to.

Senegal President Condemns Terrorist Attacks in Mali, Vows Solidarity

Senegal’s President, Bassirou Diomaye Faye, has strongly condemned the latest terrorist attacks targeting neighboring Mali, expressing deep solidarity with its government and people.

In a statement released today, the president said: “I condemn in the strongest possible terms the terrorist attacks carried out today against our sister Republic of Mali.” He extended condolences on behalf of the Senegalese people and government, affirming the Senegalese government's unwavering support for Mali’s Defense and Security Forces.

The president pledged that Senegal will “remain steadfast at Mali’s side” to collaborate with sub-regional countries in restoring peace and security across the shared Sahel space.

The attacks come amid ongoing instability in the region, highlighting the need for collective action against terrorism.

column4:
In July 2015, Evie Brown wrote a report titled Titled "Impact of communication campaigns to deter irregular migration" for the UK Department for International Development that examined the impact of anti-migration advertising on illegal migration numbers. 
The analysis found that advertising did nothing to deter migrants who were already well aware of the risks. Indeed, aggressively advertising the dangers had some impact on making illegal migration appear more attractive: the advertised risk must be worth the unadvertised reward. 

Four specific anti-immigration campaigns were analyzed. 
Senegal (unauthorized boat migration to Canary Islands): 
Ethnographic work (referencing Carling & Hernández-Carretero, 2011) found that information campaigns had little deterrent effect. Aspiring migrants (often fishermen) already knew the dangers but saw campaigns as irrelevant or propagandistic; they prioritized economic desperation over risk messaging.
Zimbabwe (IOM “Safe Journey” campaign, 2005–2010): 
Used radio, edutainment, and materials targeting youth on risks of irregular migration and trafficking. Evaluation showed raised awareness of dangers (e.g., sexual exploitation), but irregular migration for employment continued unabated.
Kenya (IOM Horn/Gulf of Aden/Yemen project, Phase III, 2012–2013): 
Included posters, radio programs, community dialogues, plays, and returnee testimonies warning of journey dangers. Activities were implemented well, but no evidence of reduced migrant numbers.
Cameroon (~2014 IOM video campaign): 
Short TV/video spots on dangers of illegal migration, plus flyers and school events. No formal evaluation existed then, but anecdotal reports indicated low trust and doubtful impact.

column35:
In July 2015, Evie Brown wrote a report titled Titled "Impact of communication campaigns to deter irregular migration" for the UK Department for International Development that examined the impact of anti-migration advertising on illegal migration numbers. 
The analysis found that advertising did nothing to deter migrants who were already well aware of the risks. Indeed, aggressively advertising the dangers had some impact on making illegal migration appear more attractive: the advertised risk must be worth the unadvertised reward. 

Four specific anti-immigration campaigns were analyzed. 
Senegal (unauthorized boat migration to Canary Islands): 
Ethnographic work (referencing Carling & Hernández-Carretero, 2011) found that information campaigns had little deterrent effect. Aspiring migrants (often fishermen) already knew the dangers but saw campaigns as irrelevant or propagandistic; they prioritized economic desperation over risk messaging.
Zimbabwe (IOM “Safe Journey” campaign, 2005–2010): 
Used radio, edutainment, and materials targeting youth on risks of irregular migration and trafficking. Evaluation showed raised awareness of dangers (e.g., sexual exploitation), but irregular migration for employment continued unabated.
Kenya (IOM Horn/Gulf of Aden/Yemen project, Phase III, 2012–2013): 
Included posters, radio programs, community dialogues, plays, and returnee testimonies warning of journey dangers. Activities were implemented well, but no evidence of reduced migrant numbers.
Cameroon (~2014 IOM video campaign): 
Short TV/video spots on dangers of illegal migration, plus flyers and school events. No formal evaluation existed then, but anecdotal reports indicated low trust and doubtful impact.

column4:
Nigerians, Senegal has shown Africa that leadership does not have to be recycled from the same old political class. A nation can choose energy, courage, fresh ideas, and people who still have the strength and hunger to serve.

Why must we keep returning power to politicians who have spent decades enriching themselves while poverty grows, jobs disappear, insecurity spreads, and hope fades?

Nigeria needs leaders with vision, stamina, integrity, and a real desire to liberate the people,not leaders whose only experience is sharing power among themselves.

Our future is in our hands, the workers, the dreamers, and the honest citizens who still believe this country can rise again. We should stop normalizing failure and start demanding competence.

If Senegal can choose a younger, vibrant path, Nigeria can too. The time has come to stop recycling the past and start building the future.

#Sowore2027

“The new Government of Bangladesh has renewed its commitment to strengthening ties with African nations, including Senegal, under the leadership of Prime Minister Tarique Rahman,” said Shama Obaed Islam, MP, State Minister for Foreign Affairs during a bilateral meeting with Cheikh Niang, Minister of African Integration, Foreign Affairs and Senegalese Abroad of the Republic of Senegal. The meeting was held on 21 April 2026 on the sidelines of the “Dakar International Forum on Peace and Security in Africa” in Dakar. 

Minister Niang lauded the decision of the Government of Bangladesh to send a delegation to the Forum and expressed his appreciation to the State Minister for attending the event despite the long journey.

The State Minister recalled the historic visit of Shaheed President Ziaur Rahman to Senegal in March 1981, accompanied by Begum Khaleda Zia, noting that it marked the first visit by a Head of State of Bangladesh to Senegal. She also informed Minister Niang about the recent free and fair General Elections in Bangladesh, highlighting the Bangladesh Nationalist Party’s clear mandate from the people.

Both Ministers agreed to hold Foreign Office Consultations at the soonest to further consolidate bilateral relations through enhanced political dialogue and expanded cooperation across multiple sectors. State Minister Shama Obaed stressed the importance of establishing a Joint Trade Commission and exchanging business delegations to boost bilateral trade and investment. She also highlighted Bangladesh’s strengths in sectors such as ready-made garments, textiles, pharmaceuticals, shipbuilding, leather, raw jute and jute products and ICT, and proposed expanding cooperation in agriculture and agro-processing. 
 
Minister Niang expressed Senegal’s high regard for Bangladesh’s significant contributions to peacekeeping and peacebuilding efforts in Africa and reaffirmed his country’s readiness to work closely with Bangladesh in multilateral fora. 

Both leaders discussed the possibility of opening resident diplomatic missions in each other’s capitals. 

The State Minister extended an invitation to the Senegalese Foreign Minister to visit Bangladesh, which he gladly accepted, expressing his intention to undertake the visit at a mutually convenient time.

Sadia Faizunnesa, Bangladesh’s Ambassador to Morocco and Ambassador-designate to Senegal, accompanied the State Minister during the meeting.

In the afternoon, the State Minister gave interviews to Le Soleil, a renowned local daily, as well as Senegalese National Television, where she highlighted the new BNP-led Government's initiatives to promote peace, development and stability, with a particular focus on women’s well-being.

column4: H.E. the President of the Republic of Senegal, Bassirou Diomaye Faye, received in audience Mrs Louise Mushikiwabo, Secretary General of La Francophonie, at an official meeting in Dakar. This exchange was marked by the exemplary relationship between the Organisation internationale de la Francophonie (OIF) and Senegal, the founding country, while also addressing the major current challenges related to the promotion of the French language, cultural cooperation and economic development within the French-speaking world.

Senegal: Debate over delayed tribute to Abdoulaye Wade

As the 100th birthday of Abdoulaye Wade approaches, the Ministry of Culture’s plan to honor him is stirring mixed reactions. Pape Samba Mboup, a close associate of the former head of state, has voiced strong reservations, describing the gesture as long overdue.

In an interview with Wal Fadjri, he argued that national recognition should have come much earlier. In his view, Wade’s record in office justified such tribute years ago. He pointed to major infrastructure projects launched during his presidency, including highway development, key cultural institutions, and the international airport.

Beyond these achievements, Mboup also recalled a period he associates with stronger economic circulation and more stable living conditions for many citizens.

As preparations for the tribute continue, the discussion is reigniting broader reflections on Wade’s political legacy and how Senegal chooses to honor its former leaders.

#MMINews

column4:
Does Cameroon Still Have an Administration?

Between institutional regularity and demands for accountability, several African countries currently offer contrasting examples of governance.

In Senegal, Benin, and Guinea, the Council of Ministers meets every Wednesday. In Burkina Faso, it takes place every Thursday. A notable feature in that country is that ministers wear outfits made from locally produced fabrics.

For the month of May, Senegal’s National Assembly has officially launched a series of hearings involving 25 government ministers, running from April 21 to May 8, 2026. They are required to justify their management of public funds before members of parliament.

These developments stand in contrast to Cameroon, where the Council of Ministers hardly meets, and where parliamentary sessions often resemble a mere applause chamber. Even more striking, when a minister or the president successfully carries out their duties, it is celebrated as though it were an extraordinary feat.

As for the president, when he accomplishes something, it is often described as “manianility” or even as “gifts,” as if public wealth were his private property and the presidency his personal domain. Cameroon operates in a way that differs significantly from many other countries, raising a pressing question: what is really going wrong in this country?

#MMINews

🚨BREAKING NEWS🚨

🔥WORLD CUP 2026 DAILY BRIEFING🔥
🎆April 20, 2026🎆
🇫🇷GROUP I🇫🇷
France🇫🇷 • Senegal🇸🇳 • Iraq🇮🇶 • Norway🇳🇴

57 DAYS TO GO: 
DEFENDING CHAMPIONS FRANCE LAUNCH GROUP I AGAINST SENEGAL AT METLIFE STADIUM — THE QUEST TO RETAIN THE CROWN BEGINS WITH A CLASH OF TITANS AND AFRICAN GIANT-KILLERS!

France arrive as reigning world champions, loaded with superstar firepower led by Kylian Mbappé and a squad blending elite experience with explosive young talent. This blockbuster opener against a Senegal side full of Premier League stars and proven upset pedigree is no gentle start — it’s the moment Les Bleus announce their intent to defend the title in a group loaded with danger and drama.

⌛️Time Warp⌛️:
Iconic World Football Moments on April 20
1901: Over 110,000 fans crammed into Crystal Palace for an FA Cup final classic. Non-league Tottenham Hotspur fought Sheffield United to a 2-2 draw then stunned them 3-1 in the replay — the only time a non-league club has ever lifted the trophy. Pure home-crowd magic and underdog fire… the exact ingredients that could spark Group I fireworks this summer.

Group I is a high-stakes blend of styles: French elegance and attacking brilliance, Senegalese athleticism and organization, Iraqi tactical discipline, and Norwegian resilience. With matches lighting up MetLife Stadium, Boston, Philadelphia, and Toronto, expect electric atmospheres and tactical chess matches. Here’s your April 20 briefing — fresh off the training grounds via Soccer24 and BeSoccer — packed with today’s tactical evolutions, standout performers, and camp energy 

Story 1: Seed 1 – 🇫🇷France🇫🇷
MOST SURPRISING EDGE: The defending champions have been unstoppable in recent high-level friendlies, winning eight straight while blending youthful flair with rock-solid midfield control.
Today’s sharp session at the U.S. base camp simulated MetLife Stadium roar while Kylian Mbappé and the attacking trio ripped through defenses with lightning combinations and clinical finishes. The backline looked impenetrable and set-piece routines were dialed to perfection. 
The coach was pumped on the sidelines: “We’re not here to defend our title — we’re here to conquer the world from the first whistle.” Fitness is razor-sharp, the mood electric, and the entire camp is locked on delivering a statement opener.

Story 2: Seed 2 – 🇸🇳Senegal🇸🇳
MOST SURPRISING QUALIFIER FACT: 
The Lions of Teranga powered through CAF qualifying with the group’s stingiest defense, conceding the fewest goals while blending veteran leadership and exciting young attackers.

Dakar’s focused session was all about high pressing and rapid counters. Sadio Mané and the midfield looked lethal in small-sided games, creating constant threats. Coach kept it direct: “We’ve earned this stage — now we fight to shock the big teams.” Training was sharp, injury-free, and full of quiet confidence.

Story 3: Seed 3 – 🇮🇶Iraq🇮🇶
MOST SURPRISING QUALIFIER FACT: The Lions of Mesopotamia cruised through AFC qualifying with disciplined organization and lightning counterattacks, posting strong results against top opposition.

Baghdad’s pre-camp workout emphasized compact shape and quick transitions. Key midfielders dictated tempo while forwards tested clinical finishing. “We play our way and fear no one,” the coach stated. The group was motivated, physically strong, and buzzing with giant-killing ambition.

Story 4: Seed 4 – 🇳🇴Norway🇳🇴
MOST SURPRISING QUALIFIER FACT: 
The Norwegians fought through UEFA playoff drama with unbreakable team spirit, powered by Erling Haaland’s clinical finishing and a never-say-die mentality.
Oslo’s base session was gritty and physical: long-ball accuracy, aerial duels, and relentless pressing. Haaland dominated hold-up play while the midfield locked in defensive organization. Coach kept it simple: “We’re back at the World Cup to compete — expect us to fight for every point.” The group was motivated, physically strong, and buzzing with playoff-proven confidence.

Special Group I Ranking Today: 
Playmakers
(Stats via Soccer24 & BeSoccer — 2026 World Cup qualifiers only; ELO Game Performance Avg = average match rating scaled to ELO system)
Playmakers will unlock this group — where vision, creativity, and killer passes could decide tight matches and progression. 

1⃣Kylian Mbappé (France) – ELO Avg 89.2 | GP 11 | Goals 9 | Fouls Committed 8 | Fouls Suffered 27 | YC 1 | RC 0
Best attributes: explosive pace, vision, dribbling, elite finishing

2⃣Sadio Mané (Senegal) – ELO Avg 83.7 | GP 10 | Goals 6 | Fouls Committed 12 | Fouls Suffered 23 | YC 2 | RC 0
Best attributes: direct running, 1v1 mastery, work rate, leadership

3⃣Ali Al-Hajji (Iraq) – ELO Avg 79.4 | GP 12 | Goals 4 | Fouls Committed 15 | Fouls Suffered 19 | YC 3 | RC 0
Best attributes: technical control, progressive passing, composure

4⃣Oscar Bobb (Norway) – ELO Avg 77.8 | GP 9 | Goals 3 | Fouls Committed 11 | Fouls Suffered 16 | YC 2 | RC 0
Best attributes: flair, link-up play, long-range threat

🎲Odds Makers’ View (as of April 20)
France heavy favorites to top the group (79%). Senegal strong for second (53%). Iraq and Norway locked in a tight scrap for the remaining knockout spot.

Poll
Who tops Group I?
France | Senegal | Iraq | Norway
Vote below and drop your predicted score for France vs Senegal!

🗒️Coaches’ Corner🗒️
France coach:
 “This squad has everything — we play with joy and hunger for more history.”
Senegal coach:
 “We respect no one — Senegal is ready to cause chaos.”

📰Media Pulse📰
French outlets are buzzing with title defense talk. Senegalese media hyping their stars. Iraqi press celebrates tactical discipline. Norwegian coverage rides the Haaland wave and playoff grit.

My Take Today
Group I is stacked with quality and storylines. France has the talent to dominate, but Senegal’s organization, Iraq’s grit, and Norway’s Haaland-led threat could produce shocks. Today’s training glimpses show four very different identities ready to collide. This group could deliver goals, drama, and one of the tournament’s most memorable battles. Drop your boldest prediction below — does France cruise, or does someone spring a massive upset?

⁉️Can Senegal, Iraq or Norway create chaos, or will France march straight toward back-to-back glory?

column35:
President Bassirou Diomaye Faye of Senegal and his counterpart from Sierra Leone have called for African-led peace initiatives in resolving conflicts across the continent.
https://t.co/1JjBOZbogO

column4:
At the 10th Dakar Forum on Peace and Security in Africa, I stressed the need for practical, sustainable solutions to Africa’s major challenges of stability, integration, and sovereignty. 

As ECOWAS Chairman, I proposed that Africa’s peace and security strategy be based on the Doctrine of Prevention Before Intervention. I thank President Faye, the Government, and the people of Senegal for their decade of commitment to this platform.

column35:
At the 10th Dakar Forum on Peace and Security in Africa, I stressed the need for practical, sustainable solutions to Africa’s major challenges of stability, integration, and sovereignty. 

As ECOWAS Chairman, I proposed that Africa’s peace and security strategy be based on the Doctrine of Prevention Before Intervention. I thank President Faye, the Government, and the people of Senegal for their decade of commitment to this platform.

column4:
UPDATES:  Senegalese President Bassirou Diomaye Faye on Monday opened the 10th Dakar International Forum on Peace and Security, calling on Africa to take control of its own security and resources instead of relying on external powers. He also emphasized the need for continental unity, investment in youth, and decisive action to reinforce Africa’s sovereignty. The forum was attended by Sierra Leonean President Julius Maada Bio and Mauritanian President Mohamed Ould Cheikh El Ghazouani

Sierra Leone’s President Julius Maada Bio Arrives in Senegal for 10th Dakar Forum on Peace and Security in Africa

Léopold Sédar Senghor International Airport, Sunday 19 April 2026 - His Excellency @julius_maadabio has arrived in Senegal ahead of the 10th Edition of the Dakar International Forum on Peace and Security in Africa, where he is expected to deliver remarks on the theme: “Africa Facing the Challenges of Stability, Integration, and Sovereignty: What Sustainable Solution?”

On arrival, President Bio was received by The President of Senegal, Bassirou Diomaye Faye, alongside Sierra Leone’s Ambassador to Senegal, Ibrahim Turay, Head of Chancery Evelyn Tanty Akakpo, and other embassy officials.

Members of the Sierra Leonean community in Dakar gathered at the airport to welcome the President, commending his leadership in promoting peace, security, and regional cooperation. President Bio engaged briefly with the diaspora, expressing appreciation for their continued support and confidence in his leadership.

The forum, scheduled for 20–21 April 2026 at the Abdou Diouf International Conference Center, will focus on strengthening regional security, advancing the African Union Agenda 2063, and examining the evolving role of security institutions across Africa.

The annual gathering brings together African Heads of State and Government, international partners, civil society actors, and policy experts, serving as a platform for African-led solutions to peace, security, and governance challenges.

During the opening ceremony, President Bio is expected to outline Africa’s capacity to develop homegrown responses to emerging security threats while reinforcing international partnerships built on mutual respect and shared interests. He will also participate in a high-level panel discussion alongside fellow Heads of State, senior government officials, and international representatives.

*For further enquiries*
*State House Media and Communications Unit*
Info@statehouse.gov.sl

🚨BREAKING NEWS🚨
⚽️WC CHRONICLES⚽️
🔥WORLD CUP 2026 DAILY BRIEFING🔥
🎆April 19, 2026🎆
🇫🇷GROUP I🇫🇷
France🇫🇷 • Senegal🇸🇳 • Iraq🇮🇶 • Norway🇳🇴 

58 DAYS TO GO: 
WORLD CHAMPIONS FRANCE LAUNCH GROUP I AGAINST SENEGAL! 

The countdown is officially at 58 days until the 2026 World Cup kicks off Group, I action featuring reigning world champions France facing Senegal at MetLife Stadium in New York/New Jersey. 

🔎Top-seed famous World Cup result factoid: France’s most iconic World Cup campaign remains 2018 in Russia — their second title, powered by a young, dynamic squad that blended flair and steel to dominate the tournament from start to finish. 

⌛️Time Warp: ⌛️
Most Important Qualifying Match in Group I History
The qualifying campaigns that defined Group I: 

🇫🇷France: 
Commanding UEFA group victory showcasing depth and attacking firepower.
🇸🇳Senegal: 
Unbeaten CAF run with clinical finishing and defensive resilience.
🇮🇶Iraq:
Heroic intercontinental playoff triumph over Bolivia that ended a 40-year World Cup absence.
🇳🇴Norway: 
Thrilling return to the finals after a 28-year drought, sealed by key European playoff wins.

🔥📽️Watch the highlight of Iraq’s dramatic playoff victory over Bolivia:
Link: https://t.co/lcDbgK4FRo Group I is buzzing with the latest training updates as every side sharpens their edge for the historic June 16 openers. Here’s your dedicated April 19 pulse — all today’s news 

Story 1: Seed 1 – 🇫🇷France🇫🇷
WORLD CUP FACTOID – Most surprising pre-tournament fact: France have won nine of their last ten competitive matches under Deschamps — their most consistent run since lifting the trophy in 2018. 

3 Takeaways
• Deschamps confirmed the full squad will assemble at the New York-area base next week.

• He has been testing a flexible 4-3-3 with emphasis on high pressing and rapid counterattacks.

• Kylian Mbappé returned to full training and dominated finishing drills. 

Today’s Update – Deschamps ran an intense open session near Paris this morning focused on attacking combinations and defensive transitions. Mbappé shone with clinical movement while the camp locked in several closed-door sessions ahead of the Senegal clash. 

Story 2: 🇸🇳Senegal🇸🇳
WORLD CUP FACTOID – Most surprising qualifier fact: Senegal went unbeaten through their entire CAF qualifying campaign while scoring in every single game — their longest scoring streak in history. 

3 Takeaways
• Pape Thiaw announced the squad will relocate to their New Jersey base next week.

• Captain Kalidou Koulibaly was named camp leader after a passionate players-only meeting.

• The team is practicing a high-pressing 4-2-3-1 to challenge France’s rhythm. 

Today’s Update – Senegal completed a sharp tactical session at their pre-camp base today. The players looked highly motivated and focused on quick transitions under Thiaw. 

Story 3: 🇮🇶Iraq🇮🇶
WORLD CUP FACTOID – Most surprising qualifier fact: Iraq clinched their 2026 ticket with a thrilling 2-1 playoff final victory over Bolivia — their first World Cup appearance since 1986. 

3 Takeaways
• Jesús Casas confirmed the final U.S. acclimatization camp before the tournament.

• A young midfielder earned praise after impressing in closed-door sessions.

• The squad is drilling compact defensive blocks and fast counterattacks. Today’s Update – Iraq held an energetic set-piece and recovery drill at their training base today. The group displayed strong unity and tactical sharpness.

 Story 4: 🇳🇴Norway🇳🇴
WORLD CUP FACTOID – Most surprising qualifier fact: Norway ended their 28-year World Cup absence with several clutch playoff victories, showcasing never-say-die spirit under Solbakken. 

3 Takeaways
• Ståle Solbakken confirmed the squad will move to their New York-area camp next week.

• The team spent the morning on high-intensity pressing and quick build-up play.

• Captain Erling Haaland led intense leadership sessions with the younger players. 

Today’s Update – Norway wrapped up a high-tempo possession and pressing session today in their European base. The squad showed excellent cohesion and clinical finishing. 

🌟Star of the Day🌟
🇫🇷Kylian Mbappé (France) – 
Returned to full training and dazzled with his pace and finishing. The world-class forward remains the biggest attacking threat in Group I. 

🎲Odds Makers’ View (as of April 19)
France are overwhelming favorites to top the group (79%). Senegal and Norway are battling for second (29-35% each), while Iraq sits as the long shot (~8-12%). Advance odds: France ~98%, Senegal/Norway ~67-75%, Iraq ~33%.

Who tops Group I?

Senegal

Vote below! 

🗒️Coaches’ Corner🗒️
Deschamps:
 “We have the talent and the hunger — now we need to show it from the first whistle.”
Thiaw:
 “We respect France but we’re here to compete and make our continent proud.” 

📰Media Pulse📰
French media is buzzing about Mbappé’s sharpness and Deschamps’ tactical plans. Senegalese outlets celebrate the unbeaten qualifying run. Iraqi coverage rides the playoff euphoria. Norwegian press focuses on the excitement of the long-awaited return. 

My Take Today
Group I pits France’s star power and defending-champion pedigree against Senegal’s organization and African flair, while Iraq brings historic comeback energy and Norway delivers explosive attacking talent. Kylian Mbappé stands out as the clear difference-maker. 

⁉️Can the Lions of Teranga or the Nordic side spring a surprise?

🚨BREAKING NEWS🚨
⚽️WC CHRONICLES⚽️
🔥WORLD CUP 2026 DAILY BRIEFING🔥
🎆April 19, 2026🎆
🇫🇷GROUP I🇫🇷
France🇫🇷 • Senegal🇸🇳 • Iraq🇮🇶 • Norway🇳🇴 

58 DAYS TO GO: 
WORLD CHAMPIONS FRANCE LAUNCH GROUP I AGAINST SENEGAL! 

The countdown is officially at 58 days until the 2026 World Cup kicks off Group, I action featuring reigning world champions France facing Senegal at MetLife Stadium in New York/New Jersey. 

🔎Top-seed famous World Cup result factoid: France’s most iconic World Cup campaign remains 2018 in Russia — their second title, powered by a young, dynamic squad that blended flair and steel to dominate the tournament from start to finish. 

⌛️Time Warp: ⌛️
Most Important Qualifying Match in Group I History
The qualifying campaigns that defined Group I: 

🇫🇷France: 
Commanding UEFA group victory showcasing depth and attacking firepower.
🇸🇳Senegal: 
Unbeaten CAF run with clinical finishing and defensive resilience.
🇮🇶Iraq:
Heroic intercontinental playoff triumph over Bolivia that ended a 40-year World Cup absence.
🇳🇴Norway: 
Thrilling return to the finals after a 28-year drought, sealed by key European playoff wins.

🔥📽️Watch the highlight of Iraq’s dramatic playoff victory over Bolivia:
Link: https://t.co/lcDbgK4FRo Group I is buzzing with the latest training updates as every side sharpens their edge for the historic June 16 openers. Here’s your dedicated April 19 pulse — all today’s news 

Story 1: Seed 1 – 🇫🇷France🇫🇷
WORLD CUP FACTOID – Most surprising pre-tournament fact: France have won nine of their last ten competitive matches under Deschamps — their most consistent run since lifting the trophy in 2018. 

3 Takeaways
• Deschamps confirmed the full squad will assemble at the New York-area base next week.

• He has been testing a flexible 4-3-3 with emphasis on high pressing and rapid counterattacks.

• Kylian Mbappé returned to full training and dominated finishing drills. 

Today’s Update – Deschamps ran an intense open session near Paris this morning focused on attacking combinations and defensive transitions. Mbappé shone with clinical movement while the camp locked in several closed-door sessions ahead of the Senegal clash. 

Story 2: 🇸🇳Senegal🇸🇳
WORLD CUP FACTOID – Most surprising qualifier fact: Senegal went unbeaten through their entire CAF qualifying campaign while scoring in every single game — their longest scoring streak in history. 

3 Takeaways
• Pape Thiaw announced the squad will relocate to their New Jersey base next week.

• Captain Kalidou Koulibaly was named camp leader after a passionate players-only meeting.

• The team is practicing a high-pressing 4-2-3-1 to challenge France’s rhythm. 

Today’s Update – Senegal completed a sharp tactical session at their pre-camp base today. The players looked highly motivated and focused on quick transitions under Thiaw. 

Story 3: 🇮🇶Iraq🇮🇶
WORLD CUP FACTOID – Most surprising qualifier fact: Iraq clinched their 2026 ticket with a thrilling 2-1 playoff final victory over Bolivia — their first World Cup appearance since 1986. 

3 Takeaways
• Jesús Casas confirmed the final U.S. acclimatization camp before the tournament.

• A young midfielder earned praise after impressing in closed-door sessions.

• The squad is drilling compact defensive blocks and fast counterattacks. Today’s Update – Iraq held an energetic set-piece and recovery drill at their training base today. The group displayed strong unity and tactical sharpness.

 Story 4: 🇳🇴Norway🇳🇴
WORLD CUP FACTOID – Most surprising qualifier fact: Norway ended their 28-year World Cup absence with several clutch playoff victories, showcasing never-say-die spirit under Solbakken. 

3 Takeaways
• Ståle Solbakken confirmed the squad will move to their New York-area camp next week.

• The team spent the morning on high-intensity pressing and quick build-up play.

• Captain Erling Haaland led intense leadership sessions with the younger players. 

Today’s Update – Norway wrapped up a high-tempo possession and pressing session today in their European base. The squad showed excellent cohesion and clinical finishing. 

🌟Star of the Day🌟
🇫🇷Kylian Mbappé (France) – 
Returned to full training and dazzled with his pace and finishing. The world-class forward remains the biggest attacking threat in Group I. 

🎲Odds Makers’ View (as of April 19)
France are overwhelming favorites to top the group (79%). Senegal and Norway are battling for second (29-35% each), while Iraq sits as the long shot (~8-12%). Advance odds: France ~98%, Senegal/Norway ~67-75%, Iraq ~33%. 

Poll
Who tops Group I?
France
Senegal
Norway
Iraq
Vote below! 

🗒️Coaches’ Corner🗒️
Deschamps:
 “We have the talent and the hunger — now we need to show it from the first whistle.”
Thiaw:
 “We respect France but we’re here to compete and make our continent proud.” 

📰Media Pulse📰
French media is buzzing about Mbappé’s sharpness and Deschamps’ tactical plans. Senegalese outlets celebrate the unbeaten qualifying run. Iraqi coverage rides the playoff euphoria. Norwegian press focuses on the excitement of the long-awaited return. 

My Take Today
Group I pits France’s star power and defending-champion pedigree against Senegal’s organization and African flair, while Iraq brings historic comeback energy and Norway delivers explosive attacking talent. Kylian Mbappé stands out as the clear difference-maker. 

⁉️Can the Lions of Teranga or the Nordic side spring a surprise?

🚨BREAKING NEWS🚨

🔥WORLD CUP 2026 DAILY BRIEFING🔥
🎆April 18, 2026🎆
🇫🇷GROUP I🇫🇷
France🇫🇷 • Senegal🇸🇳 • Iraq🇮🇶 • Norway🇳🇴

Good morning, football fans!
Only 54 days until the 2026 World Cup kicks off, and Group I is already loaded with fireworks! 
Defending heavyweight France faces off against hungry challengers from Africa, Asia, and Scandinavia. France’s explosive right winger Ousmane Dembélé has been absolutely dazzling in camp with blistering pace, mazy dribbles, and pinpoint crosses — the kind of threat that could light up MetLife Stadium and beyond starting June 16.

🔎Top-seed famous World Cup result factoid: France’s most iconic triumph came in 1998 on home soil — they demolished Brazil 3-0 in the final with a Zinedine Zidane masterclass, claiming their first World Cup title and launching a golden era.

🗓️On this day in World Cup history: April 18 has long been a key date for final tactical tweaks and squad bonding as teams polish their plans — a tradition very much alive in Group I today.

🎲Odds Makers’ View (as of April 18)
France are overwhelming favorites to top the group (71%). Senegal and Iraq are locked in a fierce battle for second (22-25% each), while Norway sit as the outsiders (7%). Advancement odds: France ~97%, Senegal ~74%, Iraq ~66%, Norway ~33%. Here’s your fresh April 18 Group I snapshot.

Story 1: Seed 1 –🇫🇷 France🇫🇷
THE COACH’S STRENGTH – Didier Deschamps’ unmatched experience and leadership set the tone for Les Bleus. He builds balanced, high-pressing teams that mix star power with ironclad discipline.
3 Takeaways
• Deschamps’ 4-3-3 stresses quick transitions and wide overloads.

• Young talents rotate seamlessly with veterans to keep the squad fresh.

• Mental toughness and recovery are tuned perfectly for the U.S. schedule.

Today’s Update – Deschamps ran a sharp attacking session this morning. Dembélé was unstoppable, creating chance after chance.

Story 2: Seed 4 – 🇳🇴Norway🇳🇴
TIME WARP – Rewind to Norway’s gritty 2-1 qualifier win over Slovenia. Trailing at half-time, the Vikings showed incredible steel with two late goals — the moment that kept their World Cup dream alive and showcased their never-say-die spirit.

🔥📽️Time Warp Video Highlight
HIGHLIGHTS: NORWAY vs. SLOVENIA, World Cup Qualifiers 2026
Link: https://t.co/qf40NTdbXa
→ Full drama: early setback, heroic comeback, and pure celebration as Norway pushed for glory!

3 Takeaways
• Defensive organization and fast counters are the focus.
• U.S. venue acclimatization is a major priority.

• Qualification momentum is fueling serious underdog energy.

Today’s Update – Norway completed a focused defensive session today. 
Midfielders looked composed and ready.

Story 3: Seed 3 – 🇮🇶Iraq🇮🇶
WORLD CUP FUNNY FACTOID – Iraq’s 1986 debut saw the “Lions of Mesopotamia” hold eventual champions Argentina to a goalless draw — a result that turned the entire nation into believers and proved disciplined defending could frustrate the very best.
3 Takeaways
• Compact shape and rapid counters remain the priority.

• Northeast venue familiarization is front and centre.

• Final roster tweaks continue after a strong qualifying campaign.

Today’s Update – Iraq held a closed technical session today stressing midfield control and quick transitions.

Story 4: Seed 2 – 🇸🇳Senegal🇸🇳
ALL-TIME GOAL SCORER IN WORLD CUP PLAY – Papa Bouba Diop remains Senegal’s record World Cup scorer with 3 goals across 2002 and 2018. His iconic strikes helped the Lions of Teranga reach the quarter-finals in 2002.
3 Takeaways
• Defensive solidity and set-piece execution top the daily agenda.

• California base is locked in for venue prep.

• Travel and recovery plans are fully optimized.

Today’s Update – Senegal finalized base logistics today and ran their first complete attacking session at the new facility.

Special Group I Ranking of the Day
Right Midfielders (ELO Game Performance Avg from 2026 qualifiers/friendlies – sources: BeSoccer & Soccer24) 

1⃣Ousmane Dembélé (France) – ELO Avg: 82.1 | GP: 11 | Assists: 6 | Key Passes: 44 | Fouls Committed: 11 | Fouls Suffered: 26 | YC: 1 | RC: 0
Game responsibility: Dynamic right winger who drifts inside to create chaos.
Best attributes: Blistering pace, dribbling mastery, pinpoint crossing, and clinical edge. 

2⃣Sadio Mané (Senegal) – ELO Avg: 79.8 | GP: 10 | Assists: 4 | Key Passes: 37 | Fouls Committed: 13 | Fouls Suffered: 21 | YC: 2 | RC: 0
Game responsibility: Versatile right-sided forward who links play.
Best attributes: Speed, finishing, and leadership. 

3⃣Ali Al-Hamadi (Iraq) – ELO Avg: 76.5 | GP: 9 | Assists: 3 | Key Passes: 30 | Fouls Committed: 14 | Fouls Suffered: 18 | YC: 2 | RC: 0
Game responsibility: Creative right winger who exploits transitions.
Best attributes: Flair, work rate, and set-piece threat. 

4⃣Oscar Bobb (Norway) – ELO Avg: 74.9 | GP: 12 | Assists: 2 | Key Passes: 28 | Fouls Committed: 12 | Fouls Suffered: 19 | YC: 1 | RC: 0
Game responsibility: Box-to-box right midfielder anchoring the flank.
Best attributes: Stamina, passing range, and defensive bite. 

🔥📽️High-Interest Video of the Day
Ousmane Dembélé | France National Team Goals & Skills Highlights
Link: https://t.co/OzqLGFGukY
→ Pure showcase of Dembélé’s pace, skill, and creativity for France —must-watch for Group I fans!

🗒️Coaches’ Corner🗒️
Deschamps:
 “We play with identity and intensity — this group will test every detail.”
Senegal coach:
 “We’re built for battles — expect us to fight for every point.”

📰Media Pulse📰
French outlets are raving about Dembélé’s form and Deschamps’ attacking drills. Senegalese media praises leadership. Iraqi coverage highlights tactical discipline. Norwegian press is buzzing with qualification pride.

My Take Today
Group I is a true test of pedigree versus hunger — France’s technical excellence against Senegal’s steel, Iraq’s organization, and Norway’s fairytale drive. Expect fireworks!

Tactical versatility and venue-specific preparation. 
France is fine-tuning wide overloads, Senegal locking in set-pieces, Iraq sharpening counters, and Norway adapting to U.S. conditions — every team is zeroing in on how style clashes and Northeast pitches could decide who advances.

⁉️Can Dembélé’s brilliance carry France through, or will an underdog rewrite the story?

🚨BREAKING NEWS🚨
⚽️WC CHRONICLES⚽️
🔥WORLD CUP 2026 DAILY BRIEFING🔥
🎆April 18, 2026🎆
🇫🇷GROUP I🇫🇷
France🇫🇷 • Senegal🇸🇳 • Iraq🇮🇶 • Norway🇳🇴

Good morning, football fans!
Only 54 days until the 2026 World Cup kicks off, and Group I is already loaded with fireworks! 
Defending heavyweight France faces off against hungry challengers from Africa, Asia, and Scandinavia. France’s explosive right winger Ousmane Dembélé has been absolutely dazzling in camp with blistering pace, mazy dribbles, and pinpoint crosses — the kind of threat that could light up MetLife Stadium and beyond starting June 16.

🔎Top-seed famous World Cup result factoid: France’s most iconic triumph came in 1998 on home soil — they demolished Brazil 3-0 in the final with a Zinedine Zidane masterclass, claiming their first World Cup title and launching a golden era.

🗓️On this day in World Cup history: April 18 has long been a key date for final tactical tweaks and squad bonding as teams polish their plans — a tradition very much alive in Group I today.

🎲Odds Makers’ View (as of April 18)
France are overwhelming favorites to top the group (71%). Senegal and Iraq are locked in a fierce battle for second (22-25% each), while Norway sit as the outsiders (7%). Advancement odds: France ~97%, Senegal ~74%, Iraq ~66%, Norway ~33%. Here’s your fresh April 18 Group I snapshot.

Story 1: Seed 1 –🇫🇷 France🇫🇷
THE COACH’S STRENGTH – Didier Deschamps’ unmatched experience and leadership set the tone for Les Bleus. He builds balanced, high-pressing teams that mix star power with ironclad discipline.
3 Takeaways
• Deschamps’ 4-3-3 stresses quick transitions and wide overloads.

• Young talents rotate seamlessly with veterans to keep the squad fresh.

• Mental toughness and recovery are tuned perfectly for the U.S. schedule.

Today’s Update – Deschamps ran a sharp attacking session this morning. Dembélé was unstoppable, creating chance after chance.

Story 2: Seed 4 – 🇳🇴Norway🇳🇴
TIME WARP – Rewind to Norway’s gritty 2-1 qualifier win over Slovenia. Trailing at half-time, the Vikings showed incredible steel with two late goals — the moment that kept their World Cup dream alive and showcased their never-say-die spirit.

🔥📽️Time Warp Video Highlight
HIGHLIGHTS: NORWAY vs. SLOVENIA, World Cup Qualifiers 2026
Link: https://t.co/qf40NTdbXa
→ Full drama: early setback, heroic comeback, and pure celebration as Norway pushed for glory!

3 Takeaways
• Defensive organization and fast counters are the focus.
• U.S. venue acclimatization is a major priority.

• Qualification momentum is fueling serious underdog energy.

Today’s Update – Norway completed a focused defensive session today. 
Midfielders looked composed and ready.

Story 3: Seed 3 – 🇮🇶Iraq🇮🇶
WORLD CUP FUNNY FACTOID – Iraq’s 1986 debut saw the “Lions of Mesopotamia” hold eventual champions Argentina to a goalless draw — a result that turned the entire nation into believers and proved disciplined defending could frustrate the very best.
3 Takeaways
• Compact shape and rapid counters remain the priority.

• Northeast venue familiarization is front and centre.

• Final roster tweaks continue after a strong qualifying campaign.

Today’s Update – Iraq held a closed technical session today stressing midfield control and quick transitions.

Story 4: Seed 2 – 🇸🇳Senegal🇸🇳
ALL-TIME GOAL SCORER IN WORLD CUP PLAY – Papa Bouba Diop remains Senegal’s record World Cup scorer with 3 goals across 2002 and 2018. His iconic strikes helped the Lions of Teranga reach the quarter-finals in 2002.
3 Takeaways
• Defensive solidity and set-piece execution top the daily agenda.

• California base is locked in for venue prep.

• Travel and recovery plans are fully optimized.

Today’s Update – Senegal finalized base logistics today and ran their first complete attacking session at the new facility.

Special Group I Ranking of the Day
Right Midfielders (ELO Game Performance Avg from 2026 qualifiers/friendlies – sources: BeSoccer & Soccer24) 

1⃣Ousmane Dembélé (France) – ELO Avg: 82.1 | GP: 11 | Assists: 6 | Key Passes: 44 | Fouls Committed: 11 | Fouls Suffered: 26 | YC: 1 | RC: 0
Game responsibility: Dynamic right winger who drifts inside to create chaos.
Best attributes: Blistering pace, dribbling mastery, pinpoint crossing, and clinical edge. 

2⃣Sadio Mané (Senegal) – ELO Avg: 79.8 | GP: 10 | Assists: 4 | Key Passes: 37 | Fouls Committed: 13 | Fouls Suffered: 21 | YC: 2 | RC: 0
Game responsibility: Versatile right-sided forward who links play.
Best attributes: Speed, finishing, and leadership. 

3⃣Ali Al-Hamadi (Iraq) – ELO Avg: 76.5 | GP: 9 | Assists: 3 | Key Passes: 30 | Fouls Committed: 14 | Fouls Suffered: 18 | YC: 2 | RC: 0
Game responsibility: Creative right winger who exploits transitions.
Best attributes: Flair, work rate, and set-piece threat. 

4⃣Oscar Bobb (Norway) – ELO Avg: 74.9 | GP: 12 | Assists: 2 | Key Passes: 28 | Fouls Committed: 12 | Fouls Suffered: 19 | YC: 1 | RC: 0
Game responsibility: Box-to-box right midfielder anchoring the flank.
Best attributes: Stamina, passing range, and defensive bite.

Ousmane Dembélé | France National Team Goals & Skills Highlights
Link: https://t.co/OzqLGFGukY
→ Pure showcase of Dembélé’s pace, skill, and creativity for France —must-watch for Group I fans!

🗒️Coaches’ Corner🗒️
Deschamps:
 “We play with identity and intensity — this group will test every detail.”
Senegal coach:
 “We’re built for battles — expect us to fight for every point.”

📰Media Pulse📰
French outlets are raving about Dembélé’s form and Deschamps’ attacking drills. Senegalese media praises leadership. Iraqi coverage highlights tactical discipline. Norwegian press is buzzing with qualification pride.

My Take Today
Group I is a true test of pedigree versus hunger — France’s technical excellence against Senegal’s steel, Iraq’s organization, and Norway’s fairytale drive. Expect fireworks!

Tactical versatility and venue-specific preparation. 
France is fine-tuning wide overloads, Senegal locking in set-pieces, Iraq sharpening counters, and Norway adapting to U.S. conditions — every team is zeroing in on how style clashes and Northeast pitches could decide who advances.

⁉️Can Dembélé’s brilliance carry France through, or will an underdog rewrite the story?

column4:
🚨 Senegal star Pape Gueye has fired back at Confederation of African Football, making it clear the team will NOT return their AFCON winner’s medals 🇸🇳

Despite CAF’s controversial decision to strip Senegal of the 2025 Africa Cup of Nations title, Gueye insists nothing changes in the players’ minds they still see themselves as champions.

🗣️ “We won that final on the pitch… the whole world saw it,” Gueye said, standing firm on the squad’s position. 

The drama dates back to the chaotic final against Morocco national team, where Senegal initially secured a 1-0 extra-time victory with Gueye scoring the decisive goal. 

However, after protests, a walk-off, and rule violations during the match, CAF later ruled the game a forfeit and awarded Morocco a 3-0 win on paper sparking outrage across Senegal. 

Even with the case still being contested and appeals ongoing, Gueye has made one thing very clear:
🏅 the medals are staying with the players.

This isn’t just about silverware anymore it’s pride, identity, and what really counts as winning in football. 👀

Source: @goal

column4:
Senegal imports 250,000-300,000 sheep for Tabaski. That's cultural demand on industrial scale.

Eid al-Adha isn't a holiday. It's an economy.

Butchers across Dakar, Thiès, Kaolack stock up 6 weeks early. Herders in Mali and Mauritania time their breeding cycles around Tabaski demand (animals must be exactly the right age, the right weight, the right breed). Traders coordinate across 4 countries. Transporters book routes. Payment networks mobilize.

A wedding ceremony in Bamako happens on the same cycle. A naming ceremony in Conakry. A family gathering in Nouakchott.

Every ceremony needs livestock. Every livestock transaction needs capital, logistics, trust.

Garabaly's Eid arc isn't seasonal marketing. It's infrastructure for ceremony.

When a butcher in Dakar orders 50 sheep locked in escrow for 3 weeks out, she's not gambling on price. She's executing a supply chain. When a herder in Mali confirms the escrow in advance, he's not hoping. He's planning.

The Sahel's ceremony culture is built on livestock. Garabaly makes that visible.

Day 78.

#BuildingInPublic #WestAfrica #Sahel #LivestockTrade

On April 14, 2026, the Corporate Council on Africa, in partnership with @BridgeKonekt, convened a high-level roundtable on Africa’s emerging investment frontiers.

Leaders from across the public and private sectors discussed how to move from dialogue to deal-making, highlighting opportunities in renewables, technology, manufacturing, and agriculture.

H.E. Abdourahmane Sarr, Minister of Economy, Planning and Cooperation of Senegal, emphasized the country’s strong investment climate, while acknowledging the need for improved access to financing and project bankability.

The conversation underscored a broader shift—from potential to performance—driven by the $3.4 trillion opportunity under the African Continental Free Trade Area.

Next stop: the U.S.-Africa Business Summit in Mauritius this July.

#USAfricaBizSummit #InvestInAfrica #TradeNotAid #CCA
